proposals.title: Discrete Gauge Symmetries and the Fate of AdS2 in Strange Metal Criticality
proposals.motivation: The AdS2 holographic description of strange metal transport relies on a continuous U(1) symmetry, which is an idealization. In real materials, lattice effects and disorder can break this to a discrete subgroup (e.g., Z_N). It is crucial to understand if the universal low-energy physics, particularly the linear-in-T resistivity and Planckian dissipation, survives this relaxation of symmetry, connecting holographic models more directly to condensed matter systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for sufficiently large N, the discrete Z_N symmetry can preserve the essential AdS2 infrared fixed point and its associated strange metal phenomenology, but that a crossover to insulating or gapped behavior occurs below a critical N, revealing a phase diagram for quantum critical transport.
proposals.method: We will construct and solve large-N Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK)-type models with complex fermions and a conserved Z_N charge. The equilibrium dynamics will be analyzed via the Schwinger-Dyson equations in the infrared limit. We will compute charge and energy transport using the ladder kernel method, extending recent work on bosonic mode localization. The low-energy effective action will be derived and compared to the gravitational theory of a charged black hole in AdS2 with a discrete gauge field.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the robustness of holographic strange metal predictions against a key microscopic constraint, providing a new theoretical framework for materials where continuous symmetry is approximate. It will yield specific, testable predictions for the temperature and frequency dependence of conductivity in models with discrete charge conservation.
proposals.novelty: While discrete symmetries are well-studied in lattice gauge theories, their role in the context of non-Fermi liquid quantum criticality and the AdS2/strange metal correspondence is largely unexplored. The systematic mapping between discrete symmetry breaking in SYK models and modifications to holographic black hole solutions is a novel direction.

proposals.title: Spectral Asymmetry and Vanishing Entropy in Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev Biliquids: A Path to Non-Fermi Liquids without a Residual Entropy
proposals.motivation: The Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model and its variants provide a solvable framework for strange metal physics, including linear-in-T resistivity and non-Fermi liquid (NFL) behavior. However, a key tension exists: the paradigmatic SYK model exhibits extensive residual entropy at zero temperature, a feature not observed in most experimental NFL candidates like cuprate strange metals. Reconciling robust NFL transport with the third law of thermodynamics is a fundamental open problem in quantum matter theory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a class of coupled SYK models, specifically 'SYK biliquids' with inter-species interactions that gap out the low-energy spectrum, can host a non-Fermi liquid phase with preserved spectral asymmetry—essential for transport—while having vanishing entropy density as T → 0.
proposals.method: We will construct and solve a model of two coupled SYK clusters (e.g., fermions with different flavors or charges) with random all-to-all interactions. The method involves: 1) Deriving the Schwinger-Dyson equations in the large-N limit to obtain the fermion self-energies and spectral functions. 2) Computing the thermodynamic entropy via the free energy derived from the effective action or directly from the partition function. 3) Using numerical solutions of the saddle-point equations (via iterative solvers) to track the entropy density S(T) and the optical conductivity σ(ω) down to very low temperatures. 4) Analyzing the spectral asymmetry via the relationship between the imaginary part of the self-energy and the particle-hole asymmetry term.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, solvable model that demonstrates the coexistence of strange metal transport and a vanishing ground state entropy, directly addressing a major conceptual hurdle in applying SYK physics to real materials. It will establish a theoretical principle for how strong interactions can destroy quasiparticles without preserving a highly degenerate ground state.
proposals.novelty: While coupled SYK models are known, the specific design goal—to explicitly engineer the destruction of residual entropy while preserving the critical NFL spectral functions and asymmetry relevant for DC and optical transport—is a novel and targeted theoretical construction beyond generic SYK studies.

proposals.title: Supersymmetric UV-Complete Fermion Models: Entanglement Entropy and Correlators in Strange Metal Regimes
proposals.motivation: The Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model and related fermionic models provide a solvable framework for non-Fermi liquid and strange metal physics, crucial for understanding high-Tc superconductivity. Supersymmetric extensions of such models are known to exhibit enhanced analytic control and non-renormalization theorems. A systematic comparison of thermodynamic and dynamical properties between SUSY and non-SUSY UV-complete fermion models could reveal the specific role of emergent supersymmetry in stabilizing quantum criticality and shaping transport anomalies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that introducing supersymmetry to a UV-complete fermion model will lead to distinct scaling of entanglement entropy and long-time correlators compared to its non-SUSY counterpart, particularly by softening infrared singularities and altering the structure of many-body chaos in the strange metal regime.
proposals.method: We will construct an N=1 supersymmetric generalization of a disordered, all-to-all interacting fermion model with a well-defined UV limit. Using large-N and replica methods, we will compute the zero-temperature entropy and finite-temperature thermodynamic potentials. Real-time correlators and out-of-time-order correlators (OTOCs) will be analyzed via the supersymmetric Schwinger-Dyson equations on the Keldysh contour. We will employ numerical solutions of these equations and compare results directly to the established non-SUSY fermion model using identical disorder averaging and scaling procedures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a concrete dictionary between supersymmetry and observable many-body features in a solvable strange metal model. It will clarify whether SUSY offers a unique mechanism for linear-in-T resistivity or modifies the Planckian dissipation rate. The results will provide a new theoretical benchmark for interpreting quantum critical transport in strongly correlated materials.
proposals.novelty: While SUSY in low-dimensional conformal field theories is well-studied, its systematic implementation and consequence in a *UV-complete*, *disordered* fermion model relevant for strange metals is unexplored. The direct, controlled comparison of entropy and dynamics in SUSY vs. non-SUSY settings with identical UV completions is a novel diagnostic for the role of enhanced symmetry.

proposals.title: Spectral Asymmetry and the Planckian Scale ω_S: A Signature of Critical Bosons in Cuprate Strange Metals
proposals.motivation: The strange metal phase of cuprates exhibits a linear-in-temperature resistivity and a universal 'Planckian' scattering rate, suggesting a fundamental quantum critical origin. Recent theoretical work, including Sachdev's analysis of overdamped bosonic modes, identifies a characteristic frequency scale ω_S ~ k_B T / ħ separating quantum critical from incoherent dynamics. Direct experimental signatures of ω_S in electronic spectra remain elusive, hindering a microscopic understanding of strange metal transport.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the crossover scale ω_S manifests as a measurable spectral asymmetry in the electronic self-energy, detectable via high-resolution spectroscopic techniques in cuprate strange metals.
proposals.method: We will employ determinant quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) simulations of the two-dimensional Hubbard model, tuned to a strange metal regime, to compute the single-particle spectral function A(k,ω). We will analyze the frequency-dependent asymmetry of A(k,ω) around the Fermi level, specifically searching for a crossover from ω/T scaling at |ω| < ω_S to a distinct high-frequency regime. This numerical signature will be compared to predictions from effective field theories of critical bosons coupled to a Fermi surface. Concurrently, we will propose specific experimental protocols for momentum-resolved electron energy-loss spectroscopy (M-EELS) and scanning tunneling spectroscopy (STS) to detect this predicted asymmetry in real materials like Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, falsifiable prediction linking the theoretical scale ω_S to a directly observable spectroscopic quantity. It will establish a new diagnostic for strange metal behavior, moving beyond transport to probe the underlying quantum critical dynamics. A positive result would strongly support the picture of strange metals as governed by a critical bosonic mode.
proposals.novelty: While ω/T scaling is widely discussed, the specific prediction of a spectral asymmetry tied to ω_S as a distinct crossover scale is novel. The proposal bridges sophisticated quantum many-body simulation with targeted, high-precision spectroscopy in a way not previously attempted for this specific signature.

proposals.title: Resolving the Residual Entropy of Quantum Spin Glasses: Ordering Transitions from Perturbations and Long-Range Interactions
proposals.motivation: The Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model and related infinite-range quantum spin glasses exhibit extensive residual entropy at zero temperature, a hallmark of a highly degenerate ground state. This entropy is central to models of strange metals and holographic duality, but its fate under realistic perturbations—such as weak spatial structure or longer-range couplings—remains unclear. Understanding how this entropy is lifted into an ordered or glassy phase is crucial for connecting universal non-Fermi liquid physics to real material systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that introducing perturbative, spatially-structured couplings or modulated long-range interactions to infinite-range quantum spin glass models will lift the extensive ground-state degeneracy, leading to a phase transition into a spin-glass or magnetically ordered state with vanishing entropy density as temperature T→0.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) and exact diagonalization for finite-N systems to map the phase diagram of perturbed SYK-like models. The core analysis will utilize the 'clustering neural quantum states via diffusion maps' technique, recently developed by the group, to identify and characterize the emergent low-temperature order parameters and the structure of the ground-state manifold. This will be complemented by analytical large-N methods to compute the low-energy density of states and identify the instability leading to entropy resolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete mechanism for how non-Fermi liquid fixed points with finite entropy evolve into conventional low-temperature phases, bridging a key gap between theoretical models and experimental observations in quantum critical systems. It will establish a new class of phase transitions driven by entropy collapse, with implications for the global phase structure of correlated electron materials.
proposals.novelty: While the residual entropy of SYK models is well-known, a systematic study of how it is resolved by perturbations relevant to real materials—using the novel framework of diffusion maps for quantum state clustering—is unexplored. This moves beyond equilibrium dynamics in pure models to a stability analysis of their defining thermodynamic feature.

proposals.title: Entanglement Scaling and Circuit Complexity in the Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev Model and Strange Metal Phases
proposals.motivation: Strange metals exhibit a robust, non-vanishing linear-in-temperature resistivity and a Planckian dissipation rate, suggesting a highly entangled quantum critical state. Pre-2026 work, including Sachdev's analysis of the Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model, has linked these properties to a finite residual entropy at zero temperature. However, a microscopic quantum information-theoretic characterization of this entropy and the associated non-Fermi liquid dynamics is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: The residual entropy of strange metals, as modeled by SYK-like systems, is directly encoded in the scaling of entanglement entropy and the exponential growth of quantum circuit complexity with system size, distinguishing it from conventional disordered or glassy phases.
proposals.method: We will employ exact diagonalization and quantum Monte Carlo methods for finite-N SYK clusters to compute the bipartite entanglement entropy (von Neumann and Rényi) across a putative strange metal to Fermi liquid transition. We will then use neural quantum state (NQS) architectures, specifically restricted Boltzmann machines, to represent ground and low-energy excited states. The quantum complexity of these NQS representations will be quantified via diffusion map analysis of their parameter manifolds, building on recent clustering techniques for NQS, to measure the intrinsic 'hardness' of the state preparation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct calculation of quantum information measures for the SYK model's strange metal phase, linking its residual entropy to concrete entanglement and complexity metrics. It will establish a new diagnostic framework, beyond traditional correlation functions, to identify and characterize strange metal behavior in numerical studies of more realistic Hubbard-like models.
proposals.novelty: While entanglement entropy is widely studied, its direct connection to the strange metal's residual entropy and the application of quantum circuit complexity measures, via analysis of neural quantum state manifolds, to this problem is a novel synthesis of quantum information and condensed matter theory.

proposals.title: Spectral Universality and Quantum Chaos in the Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev Model: A Random Matrix Theory Analysis
proposals.motivation: The Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model, an infinite-range fermionic model with random couplings, is a paradigmatic solvable model for strange metal physics and quantum black holes. Its spectral statistics are conjectured to exhibit quantum chaotic behavior, characterized by random matrix theory (RMT) universality classes, but a systematic, large-scale numerical analysis of its full many-body spectrum remains incomplete. Establishing this connection firmly is crucial for understanding the emergence of quantum chaos and ergodicity in strongly correlated systems without quasiparticles.
proposals.hypothesis: The many-body energy level statistics of the SYK model, across its full spectrum and for different numbers of Majorana fermions (N), will precisely follow the predictions of the Gaussian Orthogonal Ensemble (GOE) of random matrices, confirming its status as a maximally chaotic quantum system. Deviations at the edges of the spectrum or for specific N may reveal non-universal, model-specific features.
proposals.method: We will perform exact diagonalization of the SYK Hamiltonian for N up to ~34 Majorana fermions, focusing on the q=4 interaction. We will compute the unfolded nearest-neighbor spacing distribution, the spectral rigidity Δ3(L), and the ratio of consecutive level spacings to mitigate unfolding issues. The statistics will be compared to GOE predictions and Poisson statistics (for integrable systems) using Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests. We will also analyze the spectral form factor to probe long-range correlations, connecting to recent work on out-of-time-order correlators.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide definitive numerical evidence for RMT universality in the SYK model, solidifying its role as a minimal model of holographic quantum chaos. It will establish a precise benchmark for spectral diagnostics in non-Fermi liquid states, informing the search for SYK-like physics in experimental platforms and more realistic lattice models for strange metals.
proposals.novelty: While the SYK model's chaotic nature is widely assumed, a comprehensive RMT analysis of its *many-body* (not single-particle) spectrum across different symmetry sectors and system sizes is non-trivial and has not been fully detailed. This study directly tests the core hypothesis of its quantum chaotic character using the gold-standard tool of spectral statistics.

proposals.title: Scaling of Residual Entropy in Coupled SYK Chains: A Path to the Strange Metal Thermodynamic Limit
proposals.motivation: The Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model provides a solvable paradigm for non-Fermi liquid physics and a finite residual entropy at zero temperature, a key feature of strange metals. However, the single SYK dot lacks spatial structure, making its connection to real materials' transport and thermodynamic scaling opaque. Coupling SYK dots into extended lattices is a crucial, unresolved step toward a microscopic theory of strange metals in the thermodynamic limit.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a one-dimensional chain of coupled SYK dots, the extensive residual entropy of isolated dots will be suppressed, scaling with system size in a manner controlled by the inter-dot coupling strength, and that this scaling will reveal a crossover to a coherent strange metal phase.
proposals.method: We will construct a chain of N Majorana SYK dots, each with q=4 fermion interactions, coupled via random bilinear hopping. Using exact diagonalization for small N and quantum Monte Carlo techniques (specifically the determinant QMC method adapted for SYK-type interactions) for larger systems, we will compute the temperature-dependent entropy S(T) down to very low temperatures. We will analyze the scaling of the low-T entropy plateau with N and coupling strength, employing finite-size scaling and diffusion map analysis of quantum state correlations to identify phase structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a concrete, calculable lattice model that captures the evolution from local quantum criticality to extended non-Fermi liquid behavior. It will provide a quantitative framework for how residual entropy is resolved in a bulk system, directly informing the thermodynamic signature of strange metals. The results will bridge the gap between abstract SYK physics and the observed scaling laws in cuprates and other correlated materials.
proposals.novelty: While coupled SYK models have been proposed, a systematic finite-size study of the thermodynamic entropy scaling from a few dots to a long chain, using state-of-the-art neural quantum state and QMC methods, has not been performed. This approach directly targets the core thermodynamic puzzle of the strange metal.

proposals.title: Momentum Relaxation and Hidden Fermi Surfaces in Compressible Quantum Critical States
proposals.motivation: The strange metal phase of high-Tc cuprates and other correlated materials exhibits a linear-in-T resistivity without well-defined quasiparticles, challenging Fermi liquid theory. A key theoretical proposal is that this phase is a compressible, quantum critical state potentially hosting a hidden Fermi surface of neutral fermions, such as in the Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) models and their lattice extensions. Testing this requires probing whether signatures of this hidden Fermi surface survive the breaking of translational invariance, a necessary step to connect to real materials where momentum relaxation is intrinsic.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the thermodynamic and transport signatures of a hidden Fermi surface in a compressible quantum critical state—such as a T-linear specific heat and a finite Drude weight—are robust to weak translational symmetry breaking via lattice deformations, but will cross over to incoherent, momentum-relaxing transport at stronger disorder.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) and neural quantum state (NQS) variational methods to study a 2D lattice model of fermions coupled to critical bosons (e.g., a Yukawa-SYK-type model). Translational symmetry will be broken by introducing quasiperiodic or random lattice potential modulations. We will compute the optical conductivity, specific heat, and charge compressibility. The diffusion map technique, recently used by Sachdev's group to cluster NQS, will be adapted to analyze the structure of the many-body wavefunction and identify latent Fermi surface features in the presence of disorder.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a concrete theoretical framework for connecting translationally-invariant critical theories to realistic disordered materials. It will provide definitive numerical evidence for or against the robustness of hidden Fermi surfaces, directly informing the interpretation of strange metal phenomenology. The results will delineate the boundary between coherent 'hidden' transport and fully incoherent bad-metal behavior.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied SYK models with disorder, systematically relaxing translational invariance in compressible *critical* states to probe hidden Fermi surface signatures is unexplored. Combining NQS analysis via diffusion maps with transport calculations in such a setting is a novel methodological synthesis.

proposals.title: Hidden Fermi Surfaces and Strange Metal Transport: A Dynamical Mean-Field Theory Study of Lattice Gauge Theories
proposals.motivation: The strange metal phase of high-temperature superconductors, characterized by a linear-in-temperature resistivity and non-Fermi-liquid behavior, remains a central puzzle in quantum condensed matter physics. Pre-2026 theoretical work, including Sachdev's studies of critical spin liquids and overdamped bosonic modes, suggests that gauge-charged fermions forming a 'hidden' Fermi surface, coupled to a critical boson, could be a key ingredient. However, a controlled, non-perturbative study of such lattice models with emergent gauge fields is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a lattice model of fermions coupled to an emergent U(1) gauge field will exhibit a stable, non-Fermi-liquid phase with a hidden Fermi surface, and that Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) can capture its key transport signatures, including linear-in-T resistivity and a modified Hall angle.
proposals.method: We will employ Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) extended to treat lattice gauge theories, mapping the lattice problem onto a self-consistent quantum impurity model where the impurity is coupled to both a fermionic bath (representing the hidden Fermi surface) and a bosonic bath (representing the gauge fluctuations). The impurity solver will be a continuous-time quantum Monte Carlo (CT-QMC) method in the hybridization expansion, adapted to handle the gauge constraint. We will compute the single-particle spectral function, optical conductivity, and dc resistivity for the fermionic degrees of freedom across a range of temperatures and couplings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a numerically exact, non-perturbative benchmark for the existence and stability of hidden Fermi surface phases in a lattice setting. It will directly connect microscopic lattice gauge theory models to the phenomenology of strange metals, offering concrete predictions for transport coefficients that can be compared to experiments on cuprates and other correlated materials.
proposals.novelty: While DMFT has been applied to Hubbard models, its application to lattice gauge theories with dynamical, emergent gauge fields to study hidden Fermi surfaces is novel. This approach moves beyond effective field theory and large-N treatments to address the strong-coupling physics of gauge-matter coupling in a controlled, fully quantum framework.

proposals.title: Finite-Temperature Stability of Topological Order and Strange Metal Signatures in Quantum Spin Liquids
proposals.motivation: Topological order, a key concept in understanding quantum spin liquids and high-Tc superconductivity, is primarily defined at zero temperature. However, experiments on candidate materials like cuprates and heavy fermion systems probe finite-temperature regimes where thermal fluctuations are significant. It is unknown how topological signatures, such as long-range entanglement and emergent gauge fields, manifest or degrade at experimentally relevant temperatures, creating a critical gap between theory and observation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct signatures of topological order, particularly the deconfined phase of an emergent gauge theory, can remain observable in finite-temperature dynamical correlators and transport properties, providing a measurable link to strange metal phenomenology.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale quantum Monte Carlo and neural quantum state techniques, building on recent work with diffusion maps for state clustering, to simulate finite-temperature states of frustrated quantum spin models (e.g., Kitaev or J1-J2 models). We will compute the temperature dependence of topological entanglement entropy via replica tricks and diagnose the stability of the deconfined phase through the dynamics of emergent vison excitations. Transport properties, including the optical conductivity, will be calculated to connect topological features to strange metal linear-in-T resistivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a concrete theoretical framework for detecting topological order at finite temperature, moving beyond ground-state diagnostics. It will provide specific predictions for experimental probes like neutron scattering and transport in quantum spin liquid candidates, potentially explaining the origin of strange metal behavior as a finite-temperature consequence of proximate topological order.
proposals.novelty: While topological order is well-studied at T=0, its systematic finite-temperature characterization in realistic spin models, directly linked to strange metal transport, is largely unexplored. The combination of advanced neural quantum states and finite-temperature dynamical analysis for this purpose is novel.

proposals.title: Variational Preparation of Quantum Spin Liquids via Neural Quantum States and Measurement Feedback
proposals.motivation: The adiabatic preparation of topological spin liquids, such as the Kitaev spin liquid, is fundamentally limited by critical slowing down near quantum phase transitions, making experimental realization in quantum simulators extremely challenging. This bottleneck impedes the study of emergent anyonic excitations and their potential connection to transport anomalies in strange metals. A non-adiabatic protocol is urgently needed to access these highly entangled states on realistic timescales.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of variational neural quantum state optimization and local measurement-based feedback can efficiently prepare a topological spin liquid ground state from a trivial initial product state, bypassing the adiabatic path and its associated critical slowdown.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-stage protocol. First, we will variationally optimize a Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM) neural quantum state to approximate the target spin liquid wavefunction (e.g., for the Kitaev model) using variational Monte Carlo. Second, we will design a digital quantum circuit or analog quantum simulator sequence that uses local projective measurements of plaquette operators (e.g., flux operators) and conditional unitary feedback operations to steer the system toward the variational target state, inspired by measurement-induced phase transitions and recent work on clustering quantum states. The protocol's efficiency will be benchmarked against adiabatic preparation using exact diagonalization and tensor network simulations for small systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, non-adiabatic blueprint for preparing topological order in near-term quantum simulators, directly addressing a key experimental hurdle. It will establish a new connection between measurement-based quantum computation, variational neural states, and the dynamics of quantum critical systems, offering fresh insights into state preparation in the context of Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev-like models and strange metal dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While variational methods and measurement-based control have been studied separately, their combined application to circumvent the fundamental adiabatic bottleneck for preparing topological order is a novel synthesis. The approach explicitly leverages insights from the localization of overdamped bosonic modes and the clustering of quantum states to design a fast, feedback-driven path.

proposals.title: Strange Metal Signatures and Quantum Criticality in Three-Dimensional Topologically Ordered Atom Arrays
proposals.motivation: The search for stable, three-dimensional (3D) topological quantum spin liquids (QSLs) is a central challenge in quantum matter, with potential connections to high-temperature superconductivity and the strange metal phase. Pre-2026 experiments with Rydberg atom arrays on 2D kagome lattices have demonstrated programmable quantum spin models, but extending these to 3D geometries is crucial for realizing more robust topological order and exploring its interplay with unconventional transport phenomena.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering geometrically frustrated 3D lattices (e.g., pyrochlore or hyperkagome) with Rydberg atom arrays will stabilize a deconfined quantum spin liquid phase, and that its finite-temperature dynamics will exhibit strange metal-like transport signatures, such as a linear-in-T resistivity, near a quantum critical point.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) and neural quantum state (NQS) variational methods, specifically using autoregressive recurrent neural networks, to simulate the equilibrium dynamics of frustrated quantum Ising/XYZ models on 3D lattices. The NQS framework, enhanced by diffusion map analysis for state clustering, will be used to diagnose topological order via entanglement entropy and Wilson loop operators. We will compute dynamical correlation functions and transport coefficients via analytic continuation of QMC data to identify strange metal behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete theoretical blueprint for realizing and diagnosing 3D topological order in upcoming atom array experiments. It will establish a direct theoretical link between the microscopic spin dynamics of a 3D QSL and the macroscopic phenomenology of strange metals, offering a new platform to study quantum criticality beyond traditional materials.
proposals.novelty: While 2D atom array simulations are established, the explicit design and analysis of a 3D topological phase with programmable frustration and its connection to strange metal transport in a clean, tunable system is a novel and non-trivial extension of pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Inverse Hamiltonian Inference for Quantum Spin Liquids from Topological Signatures and Strange Metal Transport
proposals.motivation: The identification of quantum spin liquids (QSLs) in real materials is a central challenge in condensed matter physics, often relying on indirect signatures like topological entanglement entropy or emergent gauge fields. Concurrently, the strange metal phase, with its linear-in-T resistivity, is a hallmark of many correlated systems potentially hosting QSLs. A systematic framework to connect these measured topological and transport data back to the microscopic Hamiltonian parameters is critically lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: That the combination of topological data (e.g., modular matrices, entanglement spectra) and strange metal transport coefficients (e.g., resistivity, optical conductivity) provides a sufficiently rich dataset to uniquely constrain the parameters of an effective spin Hamiltonian, such as a frustrated J1-J2 model or a Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK)-like model coupled to a bath.
proposals.method: We will develop a variational inverse-problem framework. First, we will generate a large dataset of topological signatures and transport properties using neural quantum states (NQS) and quantum Monte Carlo (where possible) for candidate Hamiltonians. For the strange metal transport, we will employ the SYK-based 'overdamped boson' model for charge dynamics. The inverse mapping will be learned using a diffusion-map-inspired architecture to cluster the high-dimensional output data and a subsequent invertible neural network (INN) to regress back to Hamiltonian parameters, ensuring bijectivity where possible.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete computational tool to guide the material search for QSLs by proposing candidate microscopic parameters from experimental data. It will also forge a stronger theoretical link between the topological character of a QSL and its inevitable dissipative transport properties, advancing the understanding of strange metals.
proposals.novelty: While forward calculations of properties from Hamiltonians are standard, the inverse problem for QSLs is largely unexplored. The novel integration of *topological* data with *non-equilibrium transport* data for Hamiltonian inference represents a significant departure from typical fitting procedures focused on static spin correlations.

proposals.title: Stability of Disordered Quantum Critical Phases: Finite-N Fluctuations in the Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev Framework
proposals.motivation: Large-N theories, such as the Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model and its variants, have provided powerful solvable frameworks for describing strange metal behavior and quantum critical points. However, the stability of the predicted non-Fermi liquid and spin glass phases at physical, finite N remains an open question, as 1/N fluctuations could destabilize the large-N saddle point and alter the phase diagram relevant to real materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic 1/N corrections will reveal that certain disordered phases (e.g., marginal Fermi liquids or spin glasses) are robust, while others are destabilized by quantum fluctuations, leading to new ordered ground states or crossover scales.
proposals.method: We will employ a diagrammatic expansion around the large-N saddle point of a generalized SYK model coupled to bosonic modes, relevant for transport. The 1/N corrections will be computed using a combination of analytical Schwinger-Keldysh techniques for real-time dynamics and numerical evaluation of Feynman diagrams via Monte Carlo integration. The stability analysis will involve computing the Gaussian fluctuation determinant and examining the fate of soft modes, building on methods used in recent work on localization of overdamped bosons.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a controlled, beyond-large-N assessment of phase stability in strongly correlated models, offering a more reliable theoretical foundation for interpreting strange metal phenomenology. It will establish criteria for when large-N predictions are qualitatively accurate for physical systems with small N (e.g., N=2, 4).
proposals.novelty: While large-N phase diagrams are well-studied, a systematic finite-N correction analysis for the stability of disordered quantum critical phases is largely unexplored and non-trivial, as it requires a consistent treatment of both fermionic and bosonic fluctuations.

proposals.title: Holographic Strange Metals: A Gauge-Gravity Duality for Spinon Deconfinement and Planckian Transport
proposals.motivation: The strange metal phase of high-temperature superconductors, characterized by linear-in-temperature resistivity and a Planckian dissipation rate, remains a central mystery in quantum condensed matter physics. A leading theoretical framework posits that this phase arises from a deconfined quantum spin liquid where spinons—fractionalized spin-1/2 excitations—are liberated. However, a controlled theoretical description of the associated confinement-deconfinement transition and the resulting non-Fermi liquid transport is notoriously difficult using conventional field theory techniques.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the confinement-deconfinement transition of spinons in a frustrated magnet can be mapped to a quantum phase transition described by a strongly coupled conformal field theory (CFT), which is holographically dual to a gravitational theory in anti-de Sitter (AdS) space. The strange metal transport properties emerge from the dynamics of this gravitational dual.
proposals.method: We will construct a bottom-up holographic model where the deconfined spinon phase is dual to a charged black brane in Einstein-Maxwell-Dilaton gravity, with the dilaton field engineered to capture key scaling properties. The confining phase will be modeled by a holographic geometry without a horizon (e.g., a thermal gas). The confinement-deconfinement transition will be identified as a Hawking-Page-type transition between these spacetime geometries. We will compute the optical conductivity and DC resistivity via gauge-gravity duality, employing numerical relativity techniques to solve the bulk equations of motion and extract the boundary CFT correlation functions, focusing on obtaining linear-in-T resistivity and a universal optical conductivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, solvable model for a spinon deconfinement transition and the associated strange metal phase, yielding specific predictions for scaling functions of transport coefficients. It will establish a new dictionary between features of quantum spin liquids (like emergent gauge fields and fractionalization) and gravitational physics, offering a novel computational pathway for strongly correlated electron systems beyond the reach of perturbative methods.
proposals.novelty: While gauge-gravity duality has been applied to strange metals, a direct holographic modeling of a spinon confinement transition—explicitly linking the gravitational Hawking-Page transition to the liberation of spin-1/2 degrees of freedom—is a novel and non-obvious synthesis of Sachdev's spin liquid research program with string-theoretic AdS/CFT methods.

proposals.title: Quantum Order-from-Disorder in S=1/2 Frustrated Magnets: A Path to Strange Metal Signatures
proposals.motivation: The order-from-disorder mechanism, where quantum or thermal fluctuations select a ground state in a classically degenerate manifold, is well-established for large spins (S >> 1/2). Its fate in the extreme quantum limit of S=1/2, relevant to many candidate quantum spin liquids and strange metal parent compounds, remains unclear. Establishing its survival or failure is crucial for understanding the quantum disordered phases and anomalous transport observed in frustrated magnets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for specific frustrated S=1/2 models with extensive classical degeneracy, sub-extensive manifolds of states can be selected via quantum order-from-disorder, leading to a quantum critical point separating this ordered phase from a fully quantum disordered spin liquid.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale numerical simulations using neural quantum states (NQS), specifically restricted Boltzmann machine architectures, optimized via variational Monte Carlo to access ground states of frustrated S=1/2 Heisenberg models on triangular and pyrochlore lattices with further frustrating interactions. To analyze the resulting wavefunctions and identify order, we will apply the diffusion map technique, as recently developed by the PI, to cluster quantum states and detect symmetry breaking patterns. Dynamical properties will be probed via analytic continuation of imaginary-time correlation functions to assess potential strange metal transport.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively answer whether order-from-disorder is a viable mechanism in the S=1/2 limit, providing a new pathway to quantum criticality. It will establish a concrete link between microscopic frustration, quantum fluctuation-driven order, and the emergence of anomalous dynamical signatures, offering a new theoretical paradigm for strange metal physics in insulating magnets.
proposals.novelty: While order-from-disorder is classic, its systematic investigation in the pure S=1/2 limit using state-of-the-art NQS and unsupervised learning for state classification is novel. The direct connection to strange metal phenomenology via bosonic spinon dynamics in this context is unexplored.

proposals.title: Survival of the Spin-Fluid Phase in a Short-Range Random Heisenberg Model: A Path to Strange Metal Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Infinite-range quantum spin glass models, like the Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model and its variants, have been pivotal in generating non-Fermi liquid and strange metal phenomenology, including linear-in-T resistivity. However, their infinite-range, all-to-all couplings are a theoretical abstraction. A critical open question is whether the key anomalous transport and thermodynamic signatures survive in more realistic models with finite-range, spatially local random exchange, which would dramatically strengthen their relevance to experimental quantum materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spin-fluid phase—characterized by a finite zero-temperature entropy and a marginal Fermi liquid-like self-energy—is robust upon replacing infinite-range couplings with short-range random exchange on a two-dimensional lattice, provided the disorder is sufficiently strong and the system remains in a quantum critical regime without spin freezing.
proposals.method: We will study a 2D square-lattice Heisenberg model with Gaussian-distributed random nearest-neighbor and next-nearest-neighbor antiferromagnetic exchange couplings. The equilibrium dynamics will be probed using large-scale quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) simulations, complemented by the stochastic analytic continuation (SAC) method to extract real-frequency spectral functions. To mitigate the sign problem for frustrated random bonds, we will employ the fermion bag approach. The low-energy eigenstates will be further analyzed using neural quantum states (NQS) optimized via variational Monte Carlo, with diffusion maps applied to the learned representations to identify phase boundaries and characterize the emergent state.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a crucial bridge between highly solvable infinite-range models and realistic disordered magnets. It will determine the minimal ingredients necessary for strange metal dynamics in lattice systems and provide concrete, numerically verifiable predictions for transport and thermodynamic scaling in a model directly relevant to candidate materials like the hole-doped cuprates near the pseudogap critical point.
proposals.novelty: While the infinite-range SYK-like physics is well-studied, a systematic numerical demonstration of its survival in a purely short-range 2D setting is lacking. The combination of advanced sign-problem-free QMC, analytic continuation, and modern neural quantum state analysis for this specific model is a novel and powerful approach to this foundational problem.

proposals.title: Anisotropic Spin Dynamics and Strange Metal Transport from Broken SU(M) Symmetry
proposals.motivation: The large-M generalization of quantum spin models with SU(M) symmetry has been a powerful theoretical tool for studying strange metal physics and quantum criticality in correlated electron systems. However, the full SU(M) symmetry is an artificial simplification; real materials possess anisotropic spin interactions. Understanding how this anisotropy modifies the critical dynamics and transport properties predicted by SU(M)-symmetric theories is a crucial step towards connecting theory to experiment.
proposals.hypothesis: Introducing XXZ-type anisotropy to break the SU(M) symmetry will fundamentally alter the spectrum of overdamped bosonic modes, leading to distinct temperature and frequency scaling in dynamical spin correlations and electrical resistivity that differs from the fully symmetric large-M limit.
proposals.method: We will study a generalized Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev-type model with XXZ anisotropy using a combination of analytical large-M expansion (treating anisotropy as a perturbation) and numerical methods. The equilibrium dynamics will be analyzed via the Schwinger-Keldysh formalism. To complement this, we will employ neural quantum state variational Monte Carlo, enhanced by diffusion map clustering to identify distinct dynamical regimes, to study finite clusters and validate the large-N analytical results.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a controlled theoretical framework for anisotropic quantum criticality, directly relevant to layered cuprates and other correlated materials. It will yield specific, testable predictions for the temperature and field dependence of resistivity and optical conductivity in the strange metal regime, moving beyond the isotropic paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While large-M expansions are standard, a systematic study of the stability of strange metal dynamics against symmetry-breaking anisotropy is lacking. Combining the analytical control of the large-M limit with modern neural quantum state techniques to probe dynamics represents a novel synthesis of methods in the field.

proposals.title: Replica Symmetry Breaking and the Gapless Spectrum of the Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev Spin Fluid
proposals.motivation: The Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model and its variants provide a solvable framework for strange metal physics and non-Fermi liquid transport. A key emergent phase is the spin-fluid, characterized by a gapless bosonic spectrum and marginal Fermi liquid behavior. Prior analyses often rely on replica-diagonal or one-step replica symmetry breaking (RSB) solutions, but the potential for full Parisi-type RSB, indicative of a complex spin glass landscape, remains unexplored in this critical context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating full Parisi RSB will modify the low-energy spectral function of the spin-fluid, potentially introducing new energy scales or altering the exponent of the gapless spectrum, thereby affecting its thermodynamic and transport signatures.
proposals.method: We will employ the path-integral formulation of the SYK model with infinite-range random Heisenberg interactions. The method involves deriving the effective action for the spin bilinear (bosonic) mode, accounting for the full Parisi order parameter function q(x). We will solve the resulting saddle-point equations numerically using a population dynamics algorithm on the Parisi ultrametric tree, iterating the Schwinger-Dyson equations for the Green's functions and self-energies to convergence. The low-energy spectral density will be extracted from the imaginary-time solution via analytical continuation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the precise role of a complex glassy landscape in a canonical model of quantum criticality and strange metals. It will clarify whether the spin-fluid phase is robust to the most general form of RSB or acquires qualitatively new features, directly impacting the theoretical understanding of overdamped bosonic modes and their localization in real materials.
proposals.novelty: While Parisi RSB is well-studied in classical and quantum spin glasses, its systematic application to the critical, gapless spin-fluid phase of the SYK model is novel. It moves beyond the typical replica-diagonal treatment of strange metal dynamics to interrogate the interplay between quantum criticality and spin glass physics.

proposals.title: Stability of the Spin-Fluid State: A Systematic 1/M Expansion for the Physical SU(2) Case
proposals.motivation: The Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) and related large-M models have provided key insights into strange metal physics and non-Fermi liquid transport. The spin-fluid state, a critical phase of fractionalized spins, emerges as a stable saddle point in the large-M limit. However, its relevance to real materials with physical M=2 (SU(2) spin) is unknown, as uncontrolled approximations at finite M may destabilize the phase.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the leading 1/M corrections to the spin-fluid saddle point are perturbatively manageable and do not induce an instability, suggesting the spin-fluid's robustness as a candidate description for strange metal phenomenology in two-dimensional systems.
proposals.method: We will compute the 1/M corrections to the spin-fluid's two-particle irreducible (2PI) effective action, extending the established large-M Schwinger-Dyson framework. The corrections arise from subleading diagrams in the disorder-averaged theory, involving fluctuations of the self-energy and the Lagrange multiplier field. We will employ a combination of analytical continuum field theory and numerical solutions of the resulting integral equations on the real-frequency axis to assess the stability of the solution against relevant perturbations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first controlled, systematic assessment of the spin-fluid's stability for the physical case, bridging the gap between soluble large-N theories and real quantum materials. It will establish whether the spin-fluid's key signatures—such as linear-in-T resistivity and a critical continuum—are robust beyond the large-M limit.
proposals.novelty: While the large-M solution is well-known, a systematic finite-M expansion for the spin-fluid state has not been performed. The novelty lies in computing and analyzing the specific fluctuation modes that could become soft at M=2, moving beyond the mean-field description.

proposals.title: Classification of Gapless Spin-Fluid Fixed Points via 0+1 Dimensional Conformal Field Theory
proposals.motivation: The strange metal phase of high-temperature superconductors exhibits a gapless, non-Fermi liquid state with linear-in-temperature resistivity, suggesting proximity to a quantum critical point. A leading theoretical framework, the Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model and its variants, describes a gapless spin-fluid phase with emergent 0+1 dimensional conformal symmetry in the infrared. Classifying the possible fixed point structures of this phase is crucial for understanding universality and transport in strongly correlated quantum matter.
proposals.hypothesis: The gapless spin-fluid fixed points, as realized in generalized SYK-type models with spin and charge degrees of freedom, can be systematically classified by the representation theory of the 0+1 dimensional conformal (reparametrization) group and its breaking patterns, leading to distinct low-energy effective actions and transport signatures.
proposals.method: We will employ large-N analytical techniques on a family of SYK-like models with SU(2) spin symmetry and complex fermions to map out their infrared conformal saddle points. The classification will involve solving the associated Schwinger-Dyson equations in the conformal limit and analyzing the spectrum of four-point functions via the Bethe-Salpeter kernel. The stability of fixed points will be assessed by computing the Lyapunov exponents of out-of-time-order correlators and the leading irrelevant operators that break conformal symmetry, linking them to specific heat and conductivity scaling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic taxonomy of gapless spin-fluid phases, predicting distinct scaling forms for thermodynamic and transport coefficients near quantum criticality. It will establish a concrete connection between microscopic spin models with strange metal behavior and universal low-energy conformal field theory descriptions.
proposals.novelty: While 0+1D conformal techniques are established for the SYK model, their application to systematically classify the fixed point structure of *gapless spin-fluids*—extending beyond the canonical complex SYK paradigm to include spin degrees of freedom relevant for cuprate physics—is a novel and non-trivial development.

proposals.title: Numerically Exact SU(2) Benchmark of Large-M Theory: Quantum Monte Carlo for the Random Heisenberg Model
proposals.motivation: The Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) and related large-M theories provide a powerful analytical framework for strange metal physics and spin-liquid behavior, predicting phenomena like linear-in-T resistivity and Planckian dissipation. However, these predictions are derived in a soluble limit (e.g., large N, large M, or infinite range) distinct from the physical SU(2) spin systems believed to host such phases. A direct numerical test at the physical symmetry is crucial to validate the relevance of these paradigmatic models to real materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that key thermodynamic and transport signatures predicted by large-M analytical treatments of random Heisenberg models—such as specific heat and spin susceptibility scaling—will survive in the physical SU(2) case, as simulated by numerically exact quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) in regimes free from the sign problem.
proposals.method: We will employ stochastic series expansion (SSE) QMC with directed loop updates to simulate the SU(2) random Heisenberg model on bipartite lattices (e.g., square, honeycomb) with Gaussian-distributed exchange couplings. Careful finite-size scaling will be performed to extract low-temperature properties. We will compute specific heat, uniform susceptibility, and spin correlation functions, directly comparing results to analytical large-M predictions for the same disorder distributions and lattice geometries.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive numerical benchmark, establishing the robustness of large-M strange metal phenomenology in a physical, disordered spin system. It will either solidify the foundation for applying SYK-like models to real quantum materials or identify crucial deviations that must be accounted for, thereby guiding future theoretical developments.
proposals.novelty: While large-M theories are well-studied analytically and QMC studies of clean spin systems are common, a systematic, numerically exact QMC study designed explicitly to validate the large-M predictions for random magnets at SU(2) is a significant and missing step in the field.

proposals.title: Random Matrix Universality and Spectral Statistics of the Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev Spin-Fluid
proposals.motivation: The Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model and its spin-fluid generalizations are paradigmatic solvable models of non-Fermi liquids and strange metals, exhibiting maximal chaos and incoherent transport. A key open question is the characterization of its finite-temperature many-body spectrum beyond the low-energy conformal limit, which governs its thermodynamic and dynamic properties. Random matrix theory (RMT) provides a universal framework for classifying the spectral correlations of complex quantum systems, but its systematic application to the SYK spin-fluid ensemble remains incomplete.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the many-body eigenvalue statistics of the SYK spin-fluid Hamiltonian, across a broad range of energy scales and model parameters, will fall into one of the three standard Wigner-Dyson universality classes (GOE, GUE, GSE), and that transitions between these classes can be controlled by symmetries and the nature of the random couplings.
proposals.method: We will numerically diagonalize large ensembles of SYK and related spin-fluid Hamiltonians (with Majorana or complex fermions, and possibly SU(M) symmetry) for finite N. We will compute the nearest-neighbor and level spacing ratio distributions, spectral form factors, and number variance, comparing them to RMT predictions. The analysis will be performed across different symmetry sectors (time-reversal, particle-hole) and as a function of temperature by restricting to eigenvalues within specific energy windows. Advanced exact diagonalization techniques and, where feasible, quantum Monte Carlo methods for larger systems will be employed.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a precise RMT classification for the SYK spin-fluid spectrum, directly linking its quantum chaotic properties to universal spectral signatures. It will provide a new diagnostic for strange metal phases in such models and clarify the energy scale at which RMT behavior emerges from the conformal infrared fixed point.
proposals.novelty: While the SYK model's connection to chaos is well-known via out-of-time-order correlators, a comprehensive finite-temperature RMT analysis of its full many-body spectrum—particularly for generalized spin-fluid models relevant to strange metals—has not been fully undertaken. The novelty lies in systematically mapping the parameter space of these models onto RMT universality classes.

proposals.title: Finite-Size Signatures of the Spin-Fluid Phase in Randomly Coupled Few-Site Clusters
proposals.motivation: The strange metal phase, central to high-Tc superconductivity, is often described as a quantum spin fluid or a non-Fermi liquid with incoherent transport. Exact diagonalization (ED) studies of infinite-range models, like the Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model, capture this physics but lack spatial structure. A key open question is how local, spatially fluctuating spin interactions in finite clusters give rise to precursors of the extensive entropy and linear-in-T resistivity characteristic of the spin-fluid phase.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that small-N clusters with random, all-to-all Heisenberg or XY couplings will exhibit clear finite-size precursors of the spin-fluid phase, identifiable through a broad, low-energy density of states, extensive low-temperature entropy, and a crossover to incoherent transport dynamics.
proposals.method: We will perform exact diagonalization on clusters of N=4 to 8 sites with random, all-to-all spin-1/2 couplings (Heisenberg or XY) drawn from a Gaussian distribution. We will compute the full many-body spectrum, density of states, thermodynamic entropy S(T), and dynamical spin structure factor. To analyze the results, we will employ diffusion map techniques, as used in recent neural quantum state work, to cluster and visualize the eigenstate properties and identify regimes of quantum chaos and many-body localization. The finite-size scaling of the low-energy density of states and entropy will be compared to SYK predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a minimal, numerically exact bridge between finite-size lattice models and the infinite-range physics of strange metals. It will identify specific spectroscopic and thermodynamic signatures in small clusters that can be sought in experimental solid-state or cold-atom quantum simulators. The results will clarify the role of spatial locality and disorder in the emergence of incoherent quantum critical dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While ED of random spin models is common, the systematic search for finite-size *precursors* of the spin-fluid phase—using combined thermodynamic, spectral, and eigenstate clustering analysis—is novel. This approach connects the established SYK paradigm directly to the physics of few-site clusters with realistic couplings.

proposals.title: Bosonic Path Integral Duality and the Emergence of the Spin-Fluid Phase
proposals.motivation: The spin-fluid phase, a disordered quantum liquid of spins without long-range magnetic order, is a key theoretical construct in understanding strange metal behavior proximate to quantum critical points in high-Tc cuprates and other correlated materials. A microscopic bosonic description of this phase is lacking, limiting our ability to connect spin dynamics to observed transport anomalies like linear-in-T resistivity. Constructing such a dual description is a crucial step towards a unified theory of strange metals.
proposals.hypothesis: The spin-fluid phase can be identified as a disordered quantum liquid of overdamped bosonic modes, whose localization properties govern the anomalous charge and spin transport observed in strange metals.
proposals.method: We will construct a bosonic path integral representation for the spin degrees of freedom in a frustrated quantum magnet using a Hubbard-Stratonovich decoupling of spin-spin interactions. The resulting effective action for bosonic (spinon) fields will be analyzed using large-N and saddle-point techniques, pre-2026, to identify a stable disordered phase. We will compute the bosonic spectral function and transport coefficients using the memory matrix formalism, explicitly linking boson localization physics—as explored in Sachdev's recent work—to the spin-fluid's dc resistivity. Numerical validation will employ quantum Monte Carlo simulations on related lattice models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete field-theoretic framework for the spin-fluid phase, directly connecting its existence to the phenomenology of strange metals. It will yield new, testable predictions for optical conductivity and NMR relaxation rates in quantum critical regimes, bridging spin and charge dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While bosonic descriptions of spin liquids exist, explicitly deriving the spin-fluid as a phase of localized, overdamped bosons and using this to directly compute strange metal transport coefficients from a spin model is a novel synthesis of Sachdev's recent themes.

proposals.title: Disorder as a Tuning Parameter for Deconfined Quantum Criticality: A Numerical Study of the J-Q Model with Quenched Randomness
proposals.motivation: Deconfined quantum critical points (DQCPs) in clean systems, like the putative continuous transition between Néel and valence bond solid (VBS) orders, are a central paradigm for exotic quantum phase transitions beyond the Landau-Ginzburg-Wilson framework. However, the stability and nature of such criticality in the presence of quenched disorder, a ubiquitous feature in real materials, remains a major open question. This is crucial for connecting theoretical models to experimental systems like doped quantum magnets or candidate spin-liquid materials, where disorder is unavoidable.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that weak random-bond disorder can either destabilize the DQCP into a first-order transition or induce a new, disorder-dominated critical phase, while stronger disorder may drive the system into a quantum Griffiths or glassy regime, fundamentally altering the clean-limit criticality.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) simulations, specifically the stochastic series expansion (SSE) method, to study the 2D J-Q model with random spatial modulation of the multi-spin couplings. To mitigate the sign problem, we will focus on SU(2)-symmetric models. We will compute disorder-averaged correlation functions, Binder cumulants, and entanglement entropy to diagnose the phase transition. To connect to Sachdev's recent work on strange metals, we will also compute charge and spin transport properties in a fermionic incarnation of the disordered model (e.g., via a parton construction) to search for anomalous, possibly Planckian, dissipation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a concrete phase diagram for deconfined criticality in the presence of disorder, clarifying its stability criteria. It will provide a theoretical framework for interpreting experiments on disordered quantum magnets and may reveal novel non-Fermi liquid transport signatures near a disorder-tuned quantum critical point, bridging the study of DQCPs and strange metal physics.
proposals.novelty: While most studies treat the DQCP as a clean-limit problem, we systematically invert this perspective to treat disorder as the primary tuning knob. The combination of high-precision QMC for disordered spin models with an analysis of emergent transport anomalies directly connects two of Sachdev's core research themes.

proposals.title: Critical Scaling and Z2 Gauge Dynamics in Neural Correlates of Cognitive Mode Transitions
proposals.motivation: Understanding the neural basis of high-level cognitive flexibility, such as shifts between holistic and analytic processing, remains a major challenge. Recent fMRI studies show distinct network activations across cultural groups for these modes, suggesting a collective neural phenomenon. This presents an opportunity to apply advanced quantum critical and gauge-theoretic frameworks, developed for strongly correlated electron systems, to model the brain's functional organization as a complex, frustrated system near a critical point.
proposals.hypothesis: The transition between holistic and analytic cognitive states is governed by an emergent Z2 gauge structure in neural assemblies, manifesting as a deconfined quantum critical point with characteristic scaling laws in neural correlation functions and spectral properties.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing high-resolution fMRI time-series data from tasks inducing cognitive mode shifts. Using methods from Sachdev's recent work on neural quantum states and spin glasses, we will train Restricted Boltzmann Machines (RBMs) to represent the probability distribution of neural activation patterns. We will then compute spin-spin correlation functions and entanglement entropy from these states, searching for scaling collapse and power-law correlations indicative of criticality. The dynamics will be modeled via an effective Z2 lattice gauge theory Hamiltonian, with parameters fit to the data to test for signatures of fractionalization and topological order.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a concrete, falsifiable bridge between abstract theories of quantum criticality and empirical cognitive neuroscience. It will provide a novel physical mechanism—deconfined gauge dynamics—for cognitive flexibility. Success would pioneer a new paradigm for analyzing brain function using tools from condensed matter physics.
proposals.novelty: While spin-glass models have been applied to neuroscience, the explicit application of Z2 gauge theory and the search for deconfined criticality in human fMRI data is unprecedented. It directly connects the mathematical structure of topological order to a measurable cognitive phenomenon.

proposals.title: Magnetic Inflation: A Primary Instability Driving Fermi Surface Reconstruction and Strange Metal Behavior
proposals.motivation: In correlated electron systems like the cuprates, the interplay between magnetism, charge order, and superconductivity remains a central puzzle. The prevailing paradigm often treats magnetic order as a secondary instability of a pre-existing Fermi liquid. This proposal challenges that view, motivated by the need to explain the anomalous transport and spectral properties of strange metals, which may originate from a fundamentally reconstructed electronic landscape set by magnetism.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a strong-coupling magnetic instability at a high energy scale acts as a 'primary inflaton,' fundamentally reconstructing the electronic structure and defining the Fermi surface topology before other orders develop. This magnetic ground state provides the essential framework for understanding subsequent phenomena like strange metallicity and superconductivity.
proposals.method: We will develop a lattice gauge theory model where a strong, attractive interaction in a specific spin channel drives a magnetic condensation at a scale far above the Fermi energy. The resulting mean-field electronic structure will be analyzed using determinant Quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) and sign-problem-free methods for specific geometries. To study dynamics and transport, we will employ the Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) non-Fermi liquid framework, generalized to include the primary magnetic order, and compute overdamped bosonic mode spectra and dc resistivity via analytic continuation of imaginary-time correlation functions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete theoretical framework where strange metal transport emerges directly from a primary magnetic state, rather than as a quantum critical crossover between symmetric phases. It will establish a new hierarchy of orders in correlated materials, potentially explaining the ubiquity of magnetism in their phase diagrams and offering a new mechanism for Fermi surface reconstruction.
proposals.novelty: The proposal's core novelty is inverting the standard hierarchy of instabilities by drawing an explicit analogy to cosmological inflation, positioning magnetism as the primary cause rather than a consequence of Fermi surface formation. This contrasts with most pre-2026 models where magnetism competes with or emerges from a metallic background.

proposals.title: Primary Magnetic Instability and Fermi Surface Reconstruction in a Solvable Strange Metal Model
proposals.motivation: In cuprate and other unconventional superconductors, the standard paradigm treats magnetism as a secondary instability emerging from a parent metallic state, often a quantum spin liquid or a strange metal. However, the nature of the parent state and the driver of Fermi surface reconstruction near optimal doping remain open questions. This proposal challenges the secondary-magnetism assumption, which may obscure a more fundamental role of magnetic order in shaping non-Fermi liquid transport and superconductivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a primary magnetic quantum critical point, described by overdamped bosonic modes, can directly reconstruct a large Fermi surface into small pockets and generate strange metal phenomenology, without requiring a pre-existing exotic non-magnetic state.
proposals.method: We will construct a solvable Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK)-type lattice model where fermions are coupled to a collective bosonic magnetic order parameter with inherent quantum critical damping. Using large-N and disorder-averaging techniques, we will compute the electronic spectral function and transport coefficients. The equilibrium dynamics of the resulting state will be analyzed using real-time path integrals and compared to neural quantum state representations of the reconstructed Fermi surface, leveraging diffusion map methods to identify phase transitions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete theoretical framework where magnetism is the primary organizing principle of the phase diagram, directly linking quantum critical magnetic fluctuations to strange metal resistivity and Fermi surface topology. It will offer a new mechanism for pseudogap formation and set a foundation for understanding the interplay between such a magnetic instability and superconductivity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly constructing a model where magnetic order is the root instability, not a subsidiary effect, and demonstrating that its critical modes can self-consistently generate a strange metal with a reconstructed Fermi surface—a reversal of the conventional theoretical hierarchy.

proposals.title: Fluctuation-Driven First-Order Transitions and the Collapse of Strange Metal Criticality: An FRG Criterion
proposals.motivation: Understanding the destruction of continuous quantum critical points by fluctuations is central to explaining the phase diagrams of high-Tc cuprates, frustrated magnets, and dense nuclear matter. In strange metals, the fate of putative quantum criticality is often obscured by strong fluctuations that can render transitions first-order, complicating the interpretation of transport and thermodynamic signatures. A systematic, first-principles framework to predict this fluctuation-induced change of order is a major open problem in quantum many-body physics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a universal criterion for fluctuation-induced first-order transitions exists within the Functional Renormalization Group (FRG) formalism, manifesting as a structural instability of the RG flow—specifically, the collapse of the Wilson-Fisher fixed point and the emergence of a runaway flow in the effective potential—which can be computed for specific order parameters and symmetries relevant to correlated electron systems.
proposals.method: We will implement a truncated FRG scheme for the effective average action, focusing on bosonic order parameter fields with O(N) and cubic symmetries in d=2+ε and d=3 dimensions. The flow equations for the scale-dependent effective potential U_k(φ) will be derived and integrated numerically. We will systematically vary parameters (e.g., number of field components, symmetry-breaking terms, spatial anisotropy) to map the boundary in theory space where the Wilson-Fisher fixed point vanishes and the flow runs away, signaling a first-order transition. This analysis will be benchmarked against known results for frustrated magnets and extended to models of the strange metal critical region.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, calculable criterion for predicting the order of phase transitions in strongly fluctuating systems, moving beyond model-specific studies. It will offer a new theoretical lens to assess the stability of quantum critical points proposed for strange metals and spin liquids, directly informing the interpretation of experimental phase diagrams.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using the structural instability of the FRG flow itself as a universal diagnostic, rather than relying on perturbative ε-expansions or phenomenological arguments, to unify the description of fluctuation-driven first-order transitions across different physical contexts.

proposals.title: Gauge Fluctuations and the Stability of the Spinon Fermi Surface in FL* States: A Quantum Monte Carlo and Neural Quantum State Study
proposals.motivation: The FL* state, featuring a spinon Fermi surface coupled to an emergent U(1) gauge field, is a leading candidate theory for strange metal behavior in correlated materials like the cuprates and certain heavy-fermion systems. However, its theoretical stability is largely based on mean-field and random phase approximation (RPA) treatments, which may inadequately handle strong gauge fluctuations. A rigorous, non-perturbative assessment of these fluctuations is crucial to validate the FL* paradigm.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that beyond-mean-field gauge fluctuations in two-dimensional lattice models do not destabilize the spinon Fermi surface but instead renormalize it, leading to a stable FL* phase with characteristic non-Fermi liquid transport. Conversely, a confinement transition to a conventional ordered state may occur if fluctuations are sufficiently strong.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale sign-problem-free quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) simulations on a 2D frustrated lattice model hosting a U(1) spin liquid with a spinon Fermi surface. To directly probe gauge fluctuations and spinon confinement, we will compute the emergent magnetic flux susceptibility and the monopole correlation function. We will complement this with a variational approach using neural quantum states (NQS), specifically a restricted Boltzmann machine architecture, to represent the ground state wavefunction and compute gauge-invariant observables beyond the reach of QMC, enabling a cross-validation of results.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first numerically exact, non-perturbative test of the FL* state's stability against gauge fluctuations in a realistic setting. It will establish definitive criteria for the existence of a stable spinon Fermi surface phase and map the phase diagram near the confinement transition, offering concrete predictions for experimental signatures in candidate materials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the combined application of state-of-the-art, pre-2026 numerical techniques—sign-problem-free QMC and NQS—to directly interrogate the dynamics of an emergent gauge field, moving decisively beyond the established RPA and mean-field analyses that dominate the existing literature on FL* theory.

proposals.title: Anomalous Transport at a Quantum Critical Fermi Surface Jump: Hall Coefficient and Optical Conductivity Signatures
proposals.motivation: A central puzzle in strange metal physics is the origin of their anomalous transport properties, which often defy Fermi liquid theory. Recent theoretical work, including Sachdev's analysis of overdamped bosonic modes, suggests the possibility of a quantum phase transition where the Fermi surface volume jumps discontinuously. Such a transition, distinct from a Lifshitz transition, could be a key organizing principle for strange metals, but its precise transport signatures remain unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a quantum critical point featuring a jump in the Fermi surface volume will produce distinct, non-monotonic signatures in the Hall coefficient and a characteristic frequency-dependent scaling in the optical conductivity, which differ qualitatively from those of a conventional critical Fermi surface.
proposals.method: We will employ a large-N Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK)-type cluster model coupled to itinerant fermions, which is known to host a critical bosonic mode and can exhibit a Fermi surface jump. Using established methods from Sachdev's work on infinite-range spin glasses and strange metals, we will compute the current-current correlation functions via an analytically continued Schwinger-Keldysh formalism. The Hall coefficient will be calculated from the off-diagonal conductivity tensor within a controlled large-N expansion, while the optical conductivity will be obtained from the dynamical memory function formalism, capturing the interplay between critical bosons and the jumping Fermi surface.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first concrete theoretical predictions for transport anomalies at a Fermi surface jump transition, offering a new set of experimental benchmarks (e.g., in Hall effect and terahertz spectroscopy) to identify such a transition in quantum materials like cuprates or twisted bilayer graphene. It will establish a direct link between a non-Landau quantum critical point and the phenomenology of strange metals.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied transport in strange metals and critical Fermi surfaces, the specific calculation of Hall and optical conductivities across a *discontinuous* jump in Fermi volume, driven by a critical boson, is a novel and unexplored direction that moves beyond continuous criticality.

proposals.title: A Partitioned Solver for Gauge-DMFT: A Computational Framework for Fractionalized Fermi Liquids
proposals.motivation: Understanding strange metal behavior and unconventional superconductivity in correlated electron systems requires theories of fractionalized phases like the FL* state, where spinons couple to emergent gauge fields. Pre-2026, combining dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) with explicit, dynamical gauge fields to study such phases has been a major technical hurdle, limiting our ability to compute transport and spectral properties in a self-consistent framework.
