proposals.hypothesis: A partitioned iterative solver, inspired by fluid-structure interaction algorithms, can achieve stable numerical self-consistency between a lattice gauge theory sector and a DMFT impurity solver, enabling the first controlled dynamical simulations of FL* phases and their transitions.
proposals.method: We develop a coupled continuum-discrete framework. The U(1) or SU(2) gauge field dynamics are simulated on a spacetime lattice using Monte Carlo methods. The DMFT impurity problem, with the gauge field providing a retarded interaction, is solved using continuous-time quantum Monte Carlo (CT-QMC) or exact diagonalization. The two solvers are coupled via a partitioned scheme: the gauge sector receives the electron self-energy as a source term, while the impurity solver receives the updated gauge propagator. Self-consistency is enforced using Anderson acceleration, a technique proven for FSI, to stabilize the fixed-point iteration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first numerically exact framework for studying the finite-temperature dynamics and transport of fractionalized Fermi liquids. It will yield concrete predictions for optical conductivity and resistivity scaling in FL* models, offering a direct link between microscopic gauge theories and strange metal phenomenology.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the adaptation of robust, partitioned coupling algorithms from computational engineering to the quantum many-body problem, a non-obvious cross-disciplinary transfer that provides a systematic path to solve the long-standing gauge-DMFT consistency problem.

proposals.title: Detecting Spinon Fermi Surfaces in Strange Metals via Entanglement Entropy Scaling
proposals.motivation: A central puzzle in correlated quantum matter is the nature of the strange metal phase, where transport and thermodynamics suggest a Fermi surface without well-defined quasiparticles. Pre-2026 theories, including Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev models and U(1) spin liquids, propose a fractionalization of the electron into spinons and chargons, leading to a spinon Fermi surface. Directly distinguishing this emergent spinon surface from a conventional electron Fermi surface in numerical simulations remains a significant challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: The entanglement entropy scaling with subsystem size for a two-dimensional fermionic system will exhibit a distinct, characteristic signature—specifically, a logarithmic violation of the area law with a modified prefactor—when the underlying degrees of freedom are fractionalized spinons forming a Fermi surface, compared to the scaling for a conventional Fermi liquid.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) and variational Monte Carlo (VMC) simulations of candidate lattice models, such as the t-J model on the square lattice and the Hubbard model on the triangular lattice, across their strange metal regimes. Using the replica trick and the method of Wang and Troyer (2014), we will compute the second Rényi entanglement entropy for cylindrical geometries. To analyze complex wavefunctions, we will adapt the 'clustering neural quantum states via diffusion maps' technique from recent work to efficiently represent and sample from the many-body ground state, enabling precise entropy calculations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, calculable quantum information-theoretic diagnostic for spinon Fermi surfaces in two dimensions. It will establish a direct link between entanglement structure and the fractionalized excitations hypothesized to underpin strange metal phenomenology, offering a new numerical tool to validate theories of non-Fermi liquids.
proposals.novelty: While entanglement entropy is known to probe Fermi surfaces, its specific scaling form to discriminate between electron and spinon Fermi surfaces—an inherently gapless spin liquid—has not been systematically calculated or proposed as a definitive numerical signature for models of strange metals.

proposals.title: Disentangling Confinement from Coarse-Graining: A Dual-Order Parameter Framework for the FL* to FL Transition
proposals.motivation: In correlated electron systems like heavy-fermion metals and quantum spin liquids, a key puzzle is the apparent discrepancy between bulk thermodynamic signatures, which suggest sharp phase transitions, and local spectroscopic probes, which often reveal persistent heterogeneous order. This is exemplified by the fractionalized Fermi liquid (FL*) to heavy Fermi liquid (FL) transition, often described as a confinement transition of spinons. Interpreting finite-cluster numerical simulations of such transitions is challenging, as spatial averaging may mask underlying heterogeneity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the apparent sharp FL* to FL confinement transition, when observed at a coarse-grained scale, can emerge from a percolative crossover of spatially fragmented confined and deconfined regions, and that this can be diagnosed by a dual order parameter framework separating bulk confinement from local heterogeneity.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale quantum Monte Carlo and variational neural quantum state methods on extended Hubbard and Kondo-Heisenberg models known to host FL* and FL phases. To track heterogeneity, we will define a local, gauge-invariant 'confinement susceptibility' operator on lattice plaquettes or small clusters. We will then apply a diffusion map algorithm—recently used by Sachdev for clustering quantum states—to the high-dimensional space of these local measurements to identify distinct spatial patterns and construct a heterogeneity measure. This will be analyzed alongside the conventional bulk spinon deconfinement order parameter across the phase diagram.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new diagnostic tool to distinguish true thermodynamic confinement transitions from percolative crossovers in numerical data. It will resolve puzzles in the interpretation of finite-size simulations and offer a concrete theoretical framework to reconcile bulk transport with local probe measurements in candidate spin-liquid and strange metal materials.
proposals.novelty: The novel synthesis of confinement theory with data-science-inspired clustering techniques to explicitly quantify and track spatial heterogeneity in quantum phase transitions moves beyond standard coarse-grained field theories. This approach directly addresses the scale-dependence of order parameter measurements, a largely overlooked issue in the study of exotic criticality.

proposals.title: Confinement Transition and Dual Order Parameters in the FL* to Fermi Liquid Crossover
proposals.motivation: The strange metal phase of high-Tc cuprates is often described by the fractionalized Fermi liquid (FL*), where spinons are deconfined. The crossover to a conventional Fermi liquid (FL) at low doping is a key unsolved problem in quantum matter. Reformulating this as a confinement transition of emergent gauge fields provides a powerful new geometric and topological perspective on the breakdown of strange metallicity.
proposals.hypothesis: The FL* to FL crossover can be understood as a confinement transition of a U(1) or Z2 gauge theory, where the confinement scale is set by doping and temperature. A dual, non-local order parameter—akin to a 't Hooft loop—will exhibit singular behavior across this transition, diagnosing the change in topological order.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale quantum Monte Carlo simulations of doped Hubbard or t-J models with ring-exchange terms to tune between FL* and FL regimes. The confinement transition will be diagnosed via the scaling of Wilson and 't Hooft loop operators. To connect to strange metal transport, we will compute the optical conductivity and use the memory matrix formalism, informed by recent work on overdamped bosonic modes, to track the evolution of scattering rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a concrete confinement-deconfinement framework for the doping-driven FL* to FL crossover, providing a new order parameter description. It will directly link the loss of topological order (confinement) to the observed changes in DC resistivity and optical spectra, offering a unified explanation for the strange metal's demise.
proposals.novelty: While FL* is well-studied, its destruction via a confinement transition, diagnosed by dual non-local operators, is a novel perspective. This approach moves beyond Landau order parameters to identify the topological change underlying the strange metal's quantum criticality.

proposals.title: Holographic Emergence of Sharp Excitations at the Strange Metal to Insulator Transition
proposals.motivation: The strange metal phase of high-Tc cuprates and other correlated materials exhibits a striking non-quasiparticle transport phenomenology, including linear-in-T resistivity and Planckian dissipation. However, proximate phases like superconductors or insulators host well-defined quasiparticles. Understanding how sharp excitations can emerge from a non-quasiparticle background at a quantum phase transition is a fundamental open question in quantum matter.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that at a continuous quantum phase transition out of a strange metal, sharp quasiparticle-like excitations can emerge holographically from the decay of a continuum of non-quasiparticle states, controlled by the scaling dimensions of operators at the critical point.
proposals.method: We will employ gauge-gravity duality, specifically the Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model and its tensor model generalizations, to construct a holographic strange metal phase. We will then introduce a relevant perturbation (e.g., a symmetry-breaking field or doping) to drive a transition to an insulating or ordered phase. The spectral function of fermionic and bosonic operators will be computed numerically by solving the coupled equations of motion in the bulk gravitational theory, using established spectral methods and Chebyshev pseudospectral techniques to resolve sharp features near the phase boundary.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete theoretical framework for how quasiparticles 'crystallize' from a quantum critical continuum, linking non-Fermi liquid transport to emergent low-energy excitations. It will yield specific, testable predictions for the evolution of spectral functions and optical conductivities across such transitions, relevant to experiments on cuprates and heavy fermion compounds.
proposals.novelty: While holographic models describe strange metals and insulators separately, a controlled holographic study of the spectral evolution *across* their transition, focusing on the emergence of sharp poles from a continuum, is novel. It directly challenges the notion of strict quasiparticle absence in non-Fermi liquids by identifying the mechanism for their recovery.

proposals.title: Lattice Models of Holographic Strange Metals: From SYK Clusters to Non-Quasiparticle Transport
proposals.motivation: The Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model provides a solvable, zero-dimensional paradigm for non-Fermi liquid physics and holographic duality, capturing key strange metal phenomenology like linear-in-T resistivity. However, its extension to spatially extended, solvable lattice models remains a critical open challenge. Such models are essential to bridge the gap between holographic toy models and the realistic transport and thermodynamic properties of quantum materials.
proposals.hypothesis: By tiling SYK-like clusters with controlled inter-cluster couplings, one can construct a family of solvable, infinite-range lattice models that preserve maximal chaos and non-quasiparticle character while enabling the computation of spatially extended transport coefficients and thermodynamic scaling functions.
proposals.method: We will construct a lattice of N Majorana or complex fermion SYK clusters, each with all-to-all q-body interactions, coupled via spatially dependent, infinite-range two-body hopping. Using the replica method and Schwinger-Dyson equations in the large-N limit, we will solve for the real-frequency Green's functions and the corresponding charge/energy transport kernels. We will employ numerical solutions of the saddle-point equations, adapting techniques from quantum spin glass dynamics, to compute the optical conductivity and its temperature dependence across different coupling regimes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first class of solvable, spatially extended models that explicitly demonstrate holographic transport (e.g., Drude width ~ T) from first principles. It will provide a concrete theoretical testbed for distinguishing between possible mechanisms for strange metal behavior in lattice systems and establish a direct connection between microscopic SYK physics and macroscopic hydrodynamic transport.
proposals.novelty: While SYK models and their chains are known, the proposed infinite-range lattice of clusters is a distinct architecture designed to preserve solvability while introducing a tunable spatial structure, moving beyond the 1D chain paradigm. This approach directly targets the phenomenology of overdamped bosonic modes and their localization, a key recent focus in strange metal theory.

proposals.title: Hydrodynamic Theory of Strange Metals and Superconductor-Insulator Transitions in Three Spatial Dimensions
proposals.motivation: Recent hydrodynamic theories for strange metals, based on the diffusion of overdamped bosonic modes, have successfully described linear-in-temperature resistivity and other anomalous transport in two-dimensional systems. However, the universal applicability of these theories and their connection to the bulk superconductor-insulator transition (SIT) in three-dimensional materials remains an open and critical question. Extending this framework to d=3 is essential for understanding real materials like overdoped cuprates and certain organic superconductors, where the SIT occurs in a bulk, three-dimensional regime.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the hydrodynamic theory of incoherent bosons can be extended to three dimensions, yielding a distinct universality class for transport and critical phenomena at the bulk SIT, characterized by modified scaling exponents for conductivity and a specific heat singularity that differs from the 2D case.
proposals.method: We will generalize the effective field theory of diffusively localized bosons to three spatial dimensions, coupling them to a U(1) gauge field to describe charge transport. The critical dynamics near the SIT will be analyzed using renormalization group techniques. To test the theory's predictions for finite-temperature transport, we will employ large-scale quantum Monte Carlo simulations on 3D lattice models (e.g., Hubbard or t-J models with disorder) using the stochastic series expansion algorithm. We will compute the optical conductivity and superfluid stiffness to map the phase diagram and extract critical exponents.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive hydrodynamic framework for strange metal transport in three dimensions and a concrete theory for the bulk superconductor-insulator quantum critical point. It will yield testable predictions for scaling laws in resistivity and specific heat, directly applicable to experiments on 3D disordered superconductors. The results will unify the description of strange metal behavior across dimensionalities and clarify the role of spatial dimension in quantum critical transport.
proposals.novelty: While the hydrodynamic strange metal theory is established in 2D, its formulation and consequences in 3D are unexplored. The proposal's novelty lies in deriving the distinct 3D critical theory and using state-of-the-art quantum Monte Carlo to validate its predictions for a bulk quantum phase transition, moving beyond the 2D thin-film paradigm.

proposals.title: Hydrodynamic Transport and the Nernst Effect in a Quantum Simulator of a Disordered Bosonic Strange Metal
proposals.motivation: The strange metal phase, characterized by linear-in-temperature resistivity and anomalous thermal transport, remains a central puzzle in quantum many-body physics. Theoretical work, including Sachdev's recent analysis of overdamped bosonic modes, suggests that transport in such phases may be governed by universal hydrodynamic equations. Direct experimental tests of these hydrodynamic predictions, particularly for thermoelectric responses like the Nernst effect, are extremely challenging in solid-state materials due to complex disorder and competing orders.
proposals.hypothesis: A strongly disordered Bose-Hubbard model near the superfluid-Mott insulator transition, realized with ultracold atoms, will exhibit a regime of incoherent transport describable by charge and heat diffusion. In this regime, the Nernst signal will obey a scaling relation predicted by hydrodynamic theory, providing a direct signature of strange metallic transport.
proposals.method: We propose to design a quantum simulator using a 2D optical lattice of bosonic atoms with a superimposed quasi-random potential to induce disorder. The system will be tuned near the quantum critical point. We will implement a novel protocol to measure the Nernst coefficient: after preparing a temperature gradient via selective heating, a magnetic field gradient (synthesized via Raman transitions) will be applied to induce a transverse particle current, measured via in-situ imaging. The analysis will employ the theoretical framework of incoherent hydrodynamics, using numerical linked-cluster expansion and exact diagonalization on small clusters to compute the predicted scaling of the thermoelectric transport coefficients with temperature and disorder strength.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, clean measurement of the Nernst effect in a tunable quantum critical system, offering an unambiguous test of hydrodynamic transport theory. It will establish cold atoms as a platform for quantifying thermoelectric phenomena in strange metals, bridging the gap between abstract theory and condensed matter experiment.
proposals.novelty: While cold atoms have studied conductivity, a direct measurement of the Nernst effect—a more sensitive probe of particle-hole asymmetry and hydrodynamic flow—in a disordered bosonic quantum critical system has not been attempted. This integrates Sachdev's theoretical work on bosonic strange metals with the unique diagnostic capabilities of quantum simulation.

proposals.title: Quantum Monte Carlo Validation of Hydrodynamic Transport at the Superfluid-Insulator Transition
proposals.motivation: Recent hydrodynamic theories for strange metals, such as the 'Planckian' dissipation framework, make specific predictions for the temperature and frequency dependence of transport coefficients near quantum critical points. A key, experimentally relevant setting is the superfluid-insulator transition, but direct numerical verification of these predictions for transport in clean lattice models has been challenging due to the fermion sign problem.
proposals.hypothesis: Sign-problem-free bosonic or spin-lattice models at the superfluid-insulator quantum critical point will exhibit the universal hydrodynamic scaling of optical conductivity, characterized by a frequency-independent, linearly temperature-dependent dc resistivity and a crossover to a quantum-critical regime.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) simulations, specifically the stochastic series expansion (SSE) and worm algorithms, on sign-problem-free models like the hard-core boson Hubbard model or the transverse-field Ising model. Using analytic continuation techniques (e.g., stochastic analytic continuation or maximum entropy methods) on imaginary-time QMC data, we will compute the real-frequency optical conductivity. This will be compared directly to predictions from effective field theories and memory matrix approaches for transport at the O(2) Wilson-Fisher critical point.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first numerically exact, controlled test of hydrodynamic transport theory at a quantum critical point in a microscopic lattice model. It will establish a benchmark for the validity of effective field theory descriptions of strange metal transport and clarify the role of bosonic critical modes versus fermionic quasiparticles in generating linear-in-T resistivity.
proposals.novelty: While QMC studies of critical exponents are standard, applying it to directly validate the *dynamical transport* predictions of hydrodynamic theory for strange metals at a bosonic critical point is a novel synthesis. It leverages sign-problem-free models to access the regime of interest, bypassing limitations of fermionic simulations.

proposals.title: Spectroscopic Signatures of Deconfined Criticality: Predictions for Neutron Scattering and Raman Response in Frustrated Quantum Magnets
proposals.motivation: Deconfined quantum critical points (DQCPs) are theorized to host exotic phenomena like emergent gauge fields and fractionalized spinon excitations, but their unambiguous experimental detection in real materials remains a major challenge. This is crucial for understanding strange metal behavior and unconventional superconductivity proximate to such critical regions. Pre-2026 theoretical work has established candidate materials and frameworks, yet concrete, material-specific predictions for spectroscopic probes are lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dynamical spin and Raman response functions of frustrated quantum magnets near a DQCP will exhibit distinct, universal signatures—such as continua and power-law scaling—that directly reflect the presence of deconfined spinons and emergent gauge fluctuations.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale quantum Monte Carlo simulations for microscopic models of candidate materials (e.g., Shastry-Sutherland lattice systems) to compute imaginary-time correlation functions. These will be analytically continued to real frequency using state-of-the-art stochastic analytic continuation and machine-learning-based methods (e.g., neural quantum state-informed diffusion map sampling) to obtain the dynamical structure factor S(q,ω) and Raman susceptibility. The analysis will focus on identifying scaling collapses and spectral weight distributions characteristic of fractionalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete theoretical bridge between abstract field theories of deconfined criticality and experimental data from neutron scattering and Raman spectroscopy. It will deliver falsifiable predictions to guide the search for DQCPs in quantum magnets and clarify the connection between spin fractionalization and anomalous transport in strange metals.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond equilibrium phase diagrams to compute specific, experimentally accessible dynamical spectra for realistic frustrated spin models, integrating modern analytic continuation techniques to address the long-standing challenge of connecting numerical simulations to spectroscopy.

proposals.title: Disorder, Harris Criterion, and the Stability of Deconfined Quantum Critical Points
proposals.motivation: Deconfined quantum critical points (DQCPs), such as those between Néel and valence bond solid states, are central to understanding exotic magnetism and potential superconductivity. They evade the Landau-Ginzburg-Wilson (LGW) paradigm, raising the fundamental question of how they respond to quenched disorder. The Harris criterion, which governs the stability of conventional critical points to randomness, has not been rigorously tested for these non-LGW transitions, a crucial gap for connecting theory to real, disordered materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Harris criterion for conventional critical points does not directly apply to DQCPs, and that weak disorder either destabilizes the critical point to a first-order transition or induces a new, disorder-dominated critical regime with emergent glassy dynamics.
proposals.method: We will employ large-scale quantum Monte Carlo simulations of the J-Q model with site-dilution or random bond disorder, using the stochastic series expansion method. To analyze the critical scaling in the presence of disorder, we will compute spin and dimer correlation functions, extract critical exponents via finite-size scaling, and apply the recently developed diffusion map technique to cluster neural quantum states and identify distinct phases. We will also compute transport properties to search for signatures of strange metal behavior near the disordered critical region.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational framework for the stability of DQCPs against disorder, directly testing the applicability of the Harris criterion. It will determine the ultimate fate of deconfined criticality in realistic settings and may reveal a novel disordered quantum critical phase with potential connections to the anomalous transport seen in strange metals.
proposals.novelty: While disorder at LGW transitions is well-studied, its impact on deconfined criticality remains largely unexplored. The combination of state-of-the-art quantum Monte Carlo for disordered systems with modern data-science clustering techniques to decipher complex phase diagrams represents a novel, powerful approach to this problem.

proposals.title: Bootstrapping the Deconfined Quantum Critical Point: Conformal Constraints on Critical Exponents
proposals.motivation: The deconfined quantum critical point (DQCP), proposed as a description of continuous phase transitions in quantum magnets like the square-lattice antiferromagnet, remains a central theoretical puzzle in quantum condensed matter physics. Pre-2026 numerical studies, including quantum Monte Carlo, yield critical exponents that are challenging to reconcile with conventional Landau-Ginzburg-Wilson paradigms, suggesting an emergent conformal field theory (CFT). Precisely determining these exponents is crucial for understanding exotic phenomena like spinon deconfinement and potential connections to strange metal transport.
proposals.hypothesis: The critical field theory of the DQCP is a unitary, non-Lagrangian conformal field theory in 2+1 dimensions, and its spectrum of scaling dimensions and operator product expansion coefficients can be rigorously constrained using the numerical conformal bootstrap, yielding precise values for key critical exponents.
proposals.method: We will formulate the DQCP bootstrap problem by identifying the global symmetry (expected to be SO(5) or a subgroup thereof) and the relevant scalar primary operators (e.g., the Néel and VBS order parameters). Using the semidefinite programming solver SDPB, we will impose crossing symmetry and unitarity constraints on the four-point correlators of these operators to compute rigorous bounds on their scaling dimensions. This will be complemented by extremal functional methods to extract the spectrum of the putative CFT at the boundary of the allowed region.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, non-perturbative bounds on the critical exponents of the DQCP from first principles, offering a benchmark for numerical simulations. It will test the fundamental hypothesis of emergent conformal invariance and potentially rule out or strongly constrain candidate CFT descriptions, sharpening our understanding of deconfined criticality.
proposals.novelty: While the conformal bootstrap has been successfully applied to critical phenomena like the 3D Ising model, its application to the intrinsically dynamical, non-Lagrangian DQCP—a strongly coupled quantum critical point—is a novel and ambitious frontier that bridges high-energy theory methods with Sachdev's work on quantum criticality and strange metals.

proposals.title: Entanglement Scaling and Fractionalized Excitations at Strange Metal Critical Points via Tensor Networks
proposals.motivation: Understanding the universal structure of quantum critical wavefunctions in models relevant to strange metals and high-Tc superconductivity remains a central challenge. Conventional methods often struggle to capture the interplay of fractionalized excitations, topological order, and critical entanglement scaling in two dimensions. A direct, wavefunction-based approach is needed to characterize the non-Fermi liquid fixed points proposed in Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev-type models and related lattice systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That the critical wavefunctions of 2D quantum spin liquids and strange metal models can be efficiently represented by tensor networks with scale-invariant (MERA) or topological (PEPS) structure, and that these representations will explicitly reveal the presence of deconfined fractionalized excitations and their associated scaling dimensions.
proposals.method: We will develop numerical algorithms to optimize multi-scale entanglement renormalization ansatz (MERA) and projected entangled-pair state (PEPS) networks for critical points in 2D quantum spin models, such as the J-Q model or the Shastry-Sutherland model near deconfined criticality. The approach will combine variational optimization using automatic differentiation with analytic constraints from conformal field theory. We will specifically compute the entanglement entropy scaling and the spectrum of scaling operators to identify signatures of emergent gauge fields and fractionalized spinon or vison excitations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, wavefunction-based framework for diagnosing and characterizing non-Landau quantum critical points with topological order. It will deliver precise numerical data on scaling dimensions and operator content at these criticalities, offering a direct link between microscopic lattice models and effective field theories for strange metals and spin liquids.
proposals.novelty: While tensor networks have been applied to gapped topological phases, their systematic deployment to extract the full operator spectrum at *critical* points between such phases, particularly in 2D, is a significant and open technical challenge that goes beyond state-of-the-art density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) studies.

proposals.title: Non-perturbative fixed points and deconfined criticality: A functional RG study of the CP1 model with monopoles
proposals.motivation: The CP1 model with monopoles is a key field theory for deconfined quantum critical points (DQCPs), proposed to describe unconventional phase transitions in quantum magnets and potentially strange metals. However, its non-perturbative fixed-point structure, crucial for understanding the stability of the DQCP and its transport signatures, remains controversial. A systematic, non-perturbative analysis is needed to resolve debates between lattice simulations and analytical approaches.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a careful functional renormalization group (FRG) treatment of the CP1 model, incorporating the effects of monopole operators and gauge fluctuations, will reveal a stable critical fixed point distinct from the perturbative Wilson-Fisher one. This fixed point is expected to control a novel critical phase with specific scaling relations for spin and gauge-invariant observables.
proposals.method: We will employ the non-perturbative functional renormalization group (FRG) in its Wetterich formulation, using a truncation for the effective average action that includes the complex CP1 fields, a dynamical U(1) gauge field, and the relevant monopole operators. The flow equations will be derived and integrated numerically, using a hybrid scheme combining grid methods for momentum dependence and pseudo-spectral methods for the field dependence of the effective potential. We will specifically analyze the stability matrix at candidate fixed points to determine critical exponents.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, field-theoretic map of the fixed-point structure governing deconfined criticality, directly testing the existence and stability of the DQCP. It will yield new predictions for critical exponents and scaling functions, offering a benchmark for numerical studies of quantum spin models and a theoretical foundation for understanding anomalous transport in candidate strange metal regimes near quantum criticality.
proposals.novelty: While FRG has been applied to related models, a dedicated study focusing on the CP1 model with explicit monopole insertion is novel. This approach directly addresses the core non-perturbative issue of monopole relevance—a key uncertainty in the DQCP literature—using a modern, systematic continuum method.

proposals.title: Self-Duality and Vortex Condensation at the Deconfined Quantum Critical Point
proposals.motivation: The deconfined quantum critical point (DQCP), proposed as a description of continuous Néel-to-VBS transitions, is a central paradigm for exotic quantum criticality beyond the Landau-Ginzburg-Wilson framework. However, its field-theoretic description in terms of deconfined spinons obscures potential emergent symmetries and self-dualities that could govern universal transport and entanglement properties, which are crucial for understanding strange metal behavior proximate to such transitions.
proposals.hypothesis: The DQCP can be reformulated as a dual vortex condensation transition, revealing a hidden self-duality structure that manifests as an emergent symmetry in the critical correlators of the order parameters and their topological defects.
proposals.method: We will employ a duality analysis starting from the CP^1 model with a non-compact U(1) gauge field, mapping spinon-vortex defects to vortices in the VBS order parameter. The critical theory will be recast as a Higgs transition of these dual vortices. To test the self-duality, we will use large-scale quantum Monte Carlo simulations on square-lattice spin models hosting DQCPs, employing the stochastic series expansion (SSE) method. We will compute correlation functions of both Néel and VBS order parameters alongside their topological defects, analyzing scaling collapses to detect emergent self-duality.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel dual formulation of the DQCP, clarifying its field-theoretic structure. It will yield concrete, testable predictions for correlation function symmetries and critical exponents, offering a new lens to interpret numerical results and potential connections to anomalous transport in strange metals near quantum criticality.
proposals.novelty: While vortex condensation is known in classical XY models, applying it as a dual description to reveal self-duality at the inherently quantum DQCP is a novel theoretical angle. This approach directly connects the DQCP's topological defects to an emergent symmetry, a structure not apparent in the standard spinon formulation.

proposals.title: Deconfined Criticality in Classical 3D: A Quantum-Classical Mapping Study of the NCCP^1 Model
proposals.motivation: Deconfined quantum critical points (DQCPs), such as the proposed transition between Néel and valence bond solid orders in 2D quantum magnets, challenge the Landau-Ginzburg-Wilson paradigm by featuring emergent gauge fields and fractionalized excitations. A key open question is whether this exotic universality class has a classical statistical mechanics analog in three dimensions via the quantum-classical mapping, which would provide a more tractable setting to study its universal properties and potential connection to strange metal transport.
proposals.hypothesis: The three-dimensional classical NCCP^1 model, which is the (2+1)D DQCP mapped to imaginary time, hosts a continuous phase transition described by a conformal field theory with emergent SO(5) symmetry and a complex vector order parameter, exhibiting non-LGW critical exponents.
proposals.method: We will perform large-scale Monte Carlo simulations of the 3D classical NCCP^1 model, employing a worm algorithm to sample the constrained loop gas representation of its partition function. Critical exponents will be extracted via finite-size scaling analysis of the correlation functions and order parameters. To mitigate sign problems in related models and probe dynamics, we will adapt the neural quantum state and diffusion map techniques from recent work to construct efficient variational representations of the model's probability distribution and analyze its manifold of configurations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a concrete, simulable classical statistical mechanics model for deconfined criticality, enabling high-precision determination of its critical exponents and scaling functions. It will clarify the role of gauge fluctuations and topological defects in 3D, potentially offering new insights into the transport anomalies (e.g., linear-in-T resistivity) associated with analogous quantum critical regions.
proposals.novelty: While the 2D quantum DQCP is intensely studied, a dedicated high-precision analysis of its direct 3D classical counterpart via modern computational techniques is lacking. The application of neural quantum state methods to this classical mapping problem is a novel computational strategy to access its equilibrium properties.

proposals.title: Emergent SU(N) Gauge Fields and Strange Metal Dynamics at a Quantum Critical Point
proposals.motivation: Recent work on strange metals, particularly in Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) and related models, has shown that the critical fluctuations of a U(1) gauge field can lead to linear-in-temperature resistivity and other non-Fermi liquid signatures. However, the emergence of non-Abelian gauge fields at quantum criticality, which could describe more complex fractionalized phases and transport anomalies, remains largely unexplored theoretically. This proposal aims to generalize the successful paradigm of emergent gauge theories to the non-Abelian SU(N) case, which is a natural step for describing spin-liquid-like states with higher symmetry and potential connections to high-Tc superconductivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a quantum critical point between distinct symmetry-breaking phases can be described by an effective theory where deconfined, low-energy SU(N) gauge fields emerge, governing the universal strange metal transport and thermodynamic properties. This critical theory will yield distinct scaling signatures in resistivity and entropy compared to the U(1) case.
proposals.method: We will construct a lattice model of SU(N) spins or fermions with frustrated interactions designed to host a deconfined quantum critical point. The primary analytical tool will be a large-N generalization of the SYK approach, combined with a Schwinger-Keldysh field theory to compute real-time dynamics. To complement this and access finite-N effects, we will employ neural quantum state (NQS) variational Monte Carlo methods, specifically using restricted Boltzmann machine architectures optimized via stochastic reconfiguration, to study the ground state and low-energy excitations of the model on finite clusters.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new theoretical framework for strange metal physics based on emergent non-Abelian gauge fields, providing concrete predictions for scaling laws in resistivity, specific heat, and spin susceptibility. It will expand the classification of possible quantum critical theories relevant to correlated electron materials and offer a potential microscopic basis for phases with non-Abelian topological order.
proposals.novelty: While the emergence of U(1) gauge fields at criticality is a known concept, the systematic study of emergent SU(N) gauge dynamics and its direct connection to strange metal transport is novel. The combination of large-N SYK techniques with finite-N NQS simulations to tackle this specific problem represents a unique methodological synthesis.

ideas.title: Magnetism as the Primary Driver of Electronic Structure: An Analogy from Cosmology
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that magnetism is secondary by constructing models where magnetic order drives Fermi surface reconstruction. The analogy is drawn from cosmology, specifically the inflationary paradigm, where a primary, rapid expansion of spacetime (driven by the inflaton field) sets the initial conditions for all subsequent structure formation, rather than being a consequence of it. The adapted solution is to propose a 'magnetic inflation' scenario: a primary magnetic instability, driven by a strong coupling in a specific channel, occurs at a high energy scale and fundamentally reconstructs the electronic landscape *before* other competing orders can develop. In this framework, the emergent magnetic order defines the Fermi surface topology and quasiparticle interactions, making phenomena like charge density waves or superconductivity secondary, constrained consequences of the new primary magnetic ground state.

ideas.title: Coupled continuum-discrete simulation for gauge-DMFT
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution strategies from computational fluid-structure interaction (FSI) to the problem of combining dynamical mean-field theory with explicit gauge field dynamics for studying fractionalized Fermi liquids (FL*). In FSI, a partitioned solver couples continuum fluid dynamics to discrete structural mechanics by exchanging boundary conditions (forces/displacements) at their interface through iterative schemes. Analogously, we propose a coupled continuum-discrete simulation framework where the gauge field (treated with lattice gauge theory methods on a continuum spacetime lattice) and the DMFT impurity problem (solved with discrete quantum impurity solvers) are coupled self-consistently. The gauge field provides an effective interaction to the DMFT impurity, while the DMFT-derived electron self-energy acts as a source term back-reacting on the gauge field propagator. A partitioned iterative solver—using techniques like fixed-point iteration or Anderson acceleration from FSI—ensures consistency between the two sectors, enabling numerical study of emergent fractionalized phases and their transitions.

ideas.title: Multi-scale confinement transitions: how coarse-grained phase boundaries mask heterogeneous local order
ideas.description: This work reformulates the FL* to FL transition as a confinement transition but critically examines how the choice of coarse-graining scale — analogous to provincial-level aggregation in socioeconomic data — can mask spatially heterogeneous local order parameters within a nominally uniform phase. By constructing a dual order parameter description that separately tracks the bulk-averaged confinement signal and a sub-cell heterogeneity measure, the framework reveals that apparent sharp transitions at coarse resolution may in fact be broad crossovers composed of spatially fragmented confined and deconfined patches. This fusion yields a diagnostic tool for distinguishing true thermodynamic confinement transitions from artifacts of spatial averaging, with implications for interpreting numerical simulations on finite clusters and for understanding percolation-like phenomena in heavy-fermion and spin-liquid materials where local probe measurements disagree with bulk transport signatures.

ideas.title: Neural Signatures of Discrete Gauge Theory in Cognitive Transitions
ideas.description: This research proposes that the shift between holistic and analytic cognitive modes, as observed in fMRI across cultural groups, can be modeled as a phase transition in a Z2 fractionalized neural system. The Z2 gauge structure from condensed matter physics provides a model for discrete, topologically protected neural assemblies whose collective excitations correspond to distinct cognitive states. By analyzing fMRI data for critical scaling and correlation patterns predicted by Z2 gauge theory, we can test if cognitive mode switching exhibits signatures of magnetic frustration or spin-liquid-like neural dynamics. This bridges abstract theoretical critical behavior with empirical neural data, offering a quantitative physical theory for cognitive flexibility.

ideas.title: Systematic FRG Criterion for Fluctuation-Induced First-Order Transitions
ideas.description: This research program employs the functional renormalization group (FRG) as a unified framework to systematically determine when and why strong fluctuations destabilize continuous criticality. By analyzing the RG flow of the effective potential for various order parameters, symmetries, and dimensions, it seeks to establish a general criterion—such as the collapse of the Wilson-Fisher fixed point or the emergence of a runaway flow—that signals a fluctuation-driven first-order transition. The approach moves beyond phenomenological relaxation of assumptions to provide first-principles, calculable predictions for the order of phase transitions in complex systems, from frustrated magnets to dense nuclear matter.

ideas.title: Cultural-Collective Spinon Pairing: Mapping Regional Social Cohesion onto Fractionalized Fermi Liquid Models
ideas.description: This research applies the large-scale, regional measurement of individualism-collectivism from social media NLP (Idea A) to the theoretical study of pairing instabilities in a fractionalized Fermi liquid (Idea B). The core hypothesis is that the degree of social collectivism, quantified as a continuous variable across regions, can be mapped onto an effective 'cultural field' parameter that modulates spinon-spinon interactions within the FL* state. This allows for the investigation of whether stronger collective cultural norms, as an external sociological parameter, can induce or enhance exotic spinon pairing, leading to a new class of models for culturally-informed quantum phase diagrams. The synergy enables a data-driven, sociologically grounded approach to predicting material properties, moving beyond abstract theoretical parameters.

ideas.title: Quench-dynamics probe of Kondo breakdown using alkaline-earth optical lattices
ideas.description: This proposal replaces the equilibrium approach to cold-atom Kondo lattice simulation with a non-equilibrium quench protocol using two-orbital alkaline-earth atoms (Yb-171/173 mixtures) in optical lattices, where orbital Fano-Feshbach resonances provide independent control of the Kondo exchange coupling. Rather than cooling to the inaccessible picokelvin Kondo coherence scale, the experiment rapidly quenches the interaction strength and monitors the transient momentum distribution via time-of-flight imaging, capturing dynamical signatures of Fermi surface reconstruction at experimentally achievable temperatures of tens of nanokelvins. Complementary quantum gas microscopy provides real-space correlation measurements that distinguish the Kondo-screened large Fermi surface from the fractionalized small Fermi surface phase, enabling a definitive test of Kondo breakdown theory without requiring equilibrium ground-state preparation.

ideas.title: Cross-cultural cognitive frameworks as analogs for quantum phase transitions: Mapping collectivist-individualist spectra onto Kondo breakdown phenomenology
ideas.description: This project develops a formal mathematical correspondence between the Kondo breakdown quantum phase transition in heavy-fermion systems and the sharp collectivist-to-individualist psychological transition observed across rice-farming versus wheat-farming societies worldwide. By treating national-level psychological metrics (holistic vs. analytic thinking, in-group loyalty, self-construal) as order parameters analogous to quasiparticle residue in heavy-fermion physics, we apply renormalization group and critical scaling analyses to the global rice-wheat cultural dataset to identify whether cultural phase transitions exhibit universality classes, critical exponents, and finite-size scaling consistent with quantum criticality frameworks. Conversely, the rich cross-national dataset spanning dozens of countries with varying rice-wheat mixtures provides empirical inspiration for new tuning parameters and mixed-phase regimes in cold-atom Kondo lattice simulations, where optical lattice geometry can be continuously tuned between configurations that mimic cooperative (rice-like) and independent (wheat-like) coupling topologies. This fusion creates a new interdisciplinary field of 'sociophysics of phase transitions' that stress-tests whether universal scaling laws genuinely transcend the quantum-to-social divide, while providing both communities with fresh analytical tools and testable predictions.

ideas.title: Spectral unmixing methods from remote sensing applied to entanglement entropy decomposition of fractionalized Fermi surfaces
ideas.description: This idea transfers spectral unmixing methodology from remote sensing to the problem of detecting spinon Fermi surfaces via entanglement entropy. In hyperspectral remote sensing, a single pixel's spectrum contains mixed contributions from multiple ground materials, and blind source separation algorithms decompose the signal into constituent endmembers by exploiting their distinct spectral fingerprints across many wavelength channels. Analogously, in a spin liquid with a spinon Fermi surface coexisting with an electron Fermi surface, the entanglement entropy across a spatial bipartition receives additive contributions from both surfaces, each imprinting a characteristic dependence on the cut geometry through the Widom formula: the leading-order entanglement scales as L ln L with a coefficient determined by the number of Fermi surface crossings with the entanglement cut. By systematically rotating and reshaping the entanglement cut across many orientations and geometries—analogous to sampling across spectral channels—one builds a multi-channel dataset where each channel probes a different projection of the underlying Fermi surface structure. Applying nonnegative matrix factorization or independent component analysis to this dataset decomposes the total entanglement signal into contributions from distinct Fermi surfaces with different symmetries and sizes. The spinon Fermi surface, which may have different shape or symmetry than the electron Fermi surface, would appear as a separate endmember with its own characteristic angular dependence. This approach is particularly powerful because it does not require prior knowledge of the spinon Fermi surface geometry—it discovers the decomposition blindly, much as unsupervised unmixing recovers unknown mineral signatures from satellite data. Numerical implementation would involve computing Rényi entropies in variational Monte Carlo or DMRG for multiple cut geometries on candidate spin liquid states, then applying the unmixing analysis to the resulting entropy dataset to identify and characterize the fractionalized Fermi surface component.

ideas.title: Holographic Duality Models for Socio-Cultural Criticality in Rice-Growing Societies
ideas.description: This research applies the holographic duality methods from theoretical physics, specifically used to model non-Fermi liquid criticality and Fermi surface reconstruction, to the socio-cultural 'phase transitions' proposed by the rice-wheat theory. It posits that the intensive irrigation and cooperative labor requirements of paddy rice cultivation create a tightly coupled socio-economic system analogous to a strongly correlated quantum system, which can be modeled holographically. The framework would map the macroscopic, observable social structures (like kinship tightness or individualism-collectivism) to a boundary theory, while the microscopic, complex web of daily cooperative constraints is modeled by a gravitational bulk theory, allowing for the prediction of critical thresholds and reconstruction dynamics. This enables the testing of the rice theory's predictions in Southeast Asia not just through static comparison, but by modeling the dynamical path-dependence and potential critical instability of societies under changing agricultural or economic pressures.

ideas.title: Multi-channel cognitive fractionalization: A Kondo lattice framework for modeling holistic-analytic neural network transitions
ideas.description: This work reinterprets the holistic-versus-analytic cognitive dichotomy observed across rice-wheat agricultural regions as a many-body phase transition, mapping distinct neural processing modes onto competing Kondo screening channels in a lattice model of interconnected brain regions. Each cortical node is treated as a local moment coupled to multiple conduction channels representing parallel cognitive strategies (holistic integration vs. analytic decomposition), where overscreening in the multi-channel regime produces non-Fermi-liquid-like critical states corresponding to hybrid cognitive modes that are neither purely holistic nor analytic. The framework generates testable predictions: fMRI connectivity patterns near the cultural-cognitive transition boundary should exhibit anomalous scaling exponents and fractionalized correlation structures distinct from either pure mode, analogous to the spin-charge separation in Kondo breakdown scenarios. By grounding cultural neuroscience in the mathematical machinery of quantum phase transitions, this fusion enables a new class of quantitative models for cognitive mode-switching that move beyond descriptive neuroimaging correlations toward predictive dynamical theories of culturally shaped cognition.

ideas.title: Agro-climatic disorder as an instrument for emergent criticality in socio-economic networks
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the physics of disordered quantum critical points with causal inference techniques from economics. It proposes using historical, exogenous variation in crop suitability—modeled as a spatially correlated but quenched disorder field—as a natural instrument to test whether regional socio-economic systems exhibit signatures analogous to a non-Fermi liquid critical point. The core hypothesis is that the interaction between this 'agro-climatic disorder' and social/economic variables can drive a system to a fragile, scale-invariant critical regime, where psychological outcomes show anomalous, non-linear scaling with agricultural productivity. This approach allows for the causal identification of agriculture's effect while simultaneously testing for the emergence of universal critical phenomena in human behavior, governed by disorder.

ideas.title: Gauge-Theoretic Cultural Coevolution: Modeling Psychological Fractionalization in Agricultural Societies
ideas.description: This research synthesizes cultural coevolution and strongly correlated electron physics by treating a society's shared psychological landscape as a fractionalized Fermi liquid (FL* state). Here, individual psychological traits (e.g., future discounting, cooperation) are the emergent 'spinons' and ‘holons’, whose dynamics are governed by an explicit gauge field representing social norms and ritual practices. We adapt the Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) plus gauge theory numerical approach to simulate how the adoption of specific, historically-attested farming techniques (like irrigation vs. slash-and-burn) modifies this gauge field, driving transitions between psychologically cohesive and fragmented societal phases. The result is a first-principles, computable model that connects concrete subsistence technologies to quantitative predictions about the distribution and transmission of complex psychological phenotypes.

ideas.title: Entanglement entropy probes of collective action in socio-ecological systems
ideas.description: This research applies the concept of entanglement entropy from quantum condensed matter physics to the study of collective action in socio-ecological systems, such as irrigation-dependent agriculture. Just as entanglement measures can separate the fractionalized spinon contribution from the electron Fermi surface in a quantum spin liquid, here they are adapted to separate the genuinely cooperative, system-level social organization from the background of individual farm-level decisions. This allows for a rigorous, model-agnostic test of whether observed water management patterns in wheat (or rice) regions represent true emergent cooperation or merely coincidental alignment of individual strategies. The approach moves beyond qualitative historical or ethnographic comparison to provide a quantitative, information-theoretic metric for the 'fractionalization' of social agency within a coupled human-natural system.

ideas.title: Cultural-cognitive fluctuations and the stability of economic innovation networks
ideas.description: This research proposes that the psychological profiles associated with historical rice versus wheat cultivation create a landscape of cognitive 'gauge fields' that constrain or enable social and economic interactions within a region. We model regional innovation ecosystems as frustrated quantum spin liquids, where entrepreneurs and firms are analogous to spinons, and their collective innovative potential forms a metastable 'economic Fermi surface.' The core investigation is to determine whether strong, correlated fluctuations in these cultural-cognitive fields—analogous to gauge fluctuations beyond mean field in condensed matter—can destabilize this innovative state, leading to phase transitions toward more rigid, hierarchical, or non-innovative economic structures. This provides a formal, testable theory for why certain cultural-psychological environments foster persistent innovation while others cause it to collapse.

ideas.title: Entanglement entropy methods for detecting fractionalization in cultural coevolution networks
ideas.description: This work imports the mathematical apparatus of entanglement entropy scaling laws — specifically the logarithmic violations of area laws that signal hidden Fermi surfaces in fractionalized quantum states — into the domain of cultural evolution network models. In gene-culture coevolution models of agricultural-psychological transmission, observed behavioral traits (analogous to electrons) may arise from multiple latent transmission pathways (analogous to fractionalized spinon and holon channels) whose individual contributions are difficult to disentangle using conventional statistical methods. By mapping cultural transmission networks onto effective lattice models and computing mutual information and entanglement-like entropy measures across partitions of the population, we can detect signatures of 'fractionalized' cultural transmission where psychological traits propagate through hidden sub-channels (e.g., implicit social norms vs. explicit instruction) that individually obey distinct scaling laws. This cross-disciplinary transfer provides cultural evolution researchers with a principled, information-theoretic diagnostic for decomposing complex coevolutionary dynamics into their constituent transmission modes, going beyond correlation to reveal the structural topology of cultural inheritance.

ideas.title: Institutional Magnetism: How State-Engineered Social Structures Drive Primary Electronic Instability
ideas.description: This research constructs agent-based or network models where long-lived state institutions (e.g., imperial clan rules, corvée labor systems) are not mere background variables but the primary ordering field. Analogous to a magnetic field in a correlated electron system, these institutional constraints directly dictate the possible 'electronic' states—such as trade patterns, innovation diffusion, or social mobility—by reconstructing the underlying opportunity landscape (the socioeconomic Fermi surface). This allows us to test whether observed regional outcomes (like crop specializations) are secondary instabilities driven by this primary 'institutional magnetism,' providing a dynamical systems approach to historical political economy.

ideas.title: Entanglement entropy probes of agricultural data aggregation masks
ideas.description: This research applies the concept of entanglement entropy from quantum information theory to the problem of data aggregation in socio-economic and agricultural datasets. Just as entanglement measures can distinguish between electron and spinon Fermi surfaces in quantum materials, they can be used to probe how much information about fine-scale crop patterns or farmer psychology is lost or 'fractionalized' when data is aggregated at provincial or administrative levels. The synergy enables a rigorous, information-theoretic quantification of within-province heterogeneity, revealing whether aggregated provincial data masks distinct, coherent sub-regions or represents a genuinely averaged state. This provides a new diagnostic tool for policy and modeling, identifying scales at which aggregation becomes misleading.

ideas.title: Neural Criticality and Cultural Cognition: Testing for First-Order Transitions in Brain State Dynamics
ideas.description: This research fuses the physics of phase transitions with cultural neuroscience to propose a new model of brain state dynamics. It hypothesizes that the shift between holistic and analytic cognitive modes, as influenced by long-term cultural practices (e.g., rice vs. wheat farming), may not be a smooth, continuous neural adjustment but a sudden, first-order transition driven by internal neural fluctuations and external task demands. Using fMRI combined with computational modeling of neural criticality, the project will design tasks that parametrically push cognitive systems to identify potential hysteresis and discontinuous jumps in neural network signatures. This tests a fundamental mechanism for how entrenched cultural cognition is maintained and switched at the neural level, offering a dynamical systems alternative to static neuroimaging correlates.

ideas.title: Cultural Trait Spectroscopy via Social Media Lexical Absorption
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core analytical framework of absorption spectroscopy from analytical chemistry to computational cultural analysis. In the analogous field, a material's composition is identified by the unique pattern of light wavelengths it absorbs. The adapted solution is to treat regional corpora of Weibo posts as the 'sample' and pre-validated lexicons for individualism and collectivism as the 'characteristic wavelengths.' By applying computational linguistics to measure the intensity of usage (i.e., 'absorption') of these signature lexical items, we can generate a quantitative 'cultural trait spectrum' for each region. This method allows for the scalable, continuous measurement of the relative presence of individualism and collectivism across China's rice and wheat farming regions, moving beyond binary classification to a spectroscopic profile of cultural composition.

ideas.title: Subnational Geospatial Analysis of Historical Cereal Ecology and Individual Psychological Outcomes
ideas.description: This study moves beyond simplistic national averages to test the rice-wheat hypothesis at the individual level within a global geospatial framework. It links geo-referenced psychological survey data from projects like the World Values Survey with historical data on the suitability and prevalence of rice versus wheat cultivation in individuals' ancestral regions. The analysis employs hierarchical Bayesian models to account for nested data structures and controls for a comprehensive set of subnational confounders, including climate, terrain, irrigation potential, and modern economic development. This design isolates the potential ecological legacy of staple crops from other regional factors and avoids the ecological fallacy.

ideas.title: Paired Discontinuity Design with Placebo Boundaries for Causal Identification at the Rice-Wheat Frontier
ideas.description: This research employs a high-resolution, multi-boundary geographic design to isolate the causal effect of rice versus wheat cultivation. It maps the rice-wheat border and classifies segments by the plausibility of exogenous border formation using historical land use and soil data. The core analysis implements a difference-in-discontinuities model, comparing outcome jumps at true crop borders to jumps at carefully matched placebo boundaries within the same landscape. This system directly tests for and subtracts out confounding from underlying spatial gradients, yielding a far more robust causal estimate than a standard spatial RDD.

ideas.title: Disentangling Historical and Agricultural Legacies via NLP of Regional Social Media
ideas.description: This research integrates historical institutional data on imperial Chinese administrative structures and kinship systems with NLP analysis of regional Weibo posts. By mapping historical state capacity and kinship intensity—which co-varied with, but were not perfectly determined by, rice/wheat zones—onto contemporary linguistic markers of individualism-collectivism, the study can statistically isolate their unique effects. This allows for a direct test of whether observed psychological differences are better explained by persistent institutional legacies or by ongoing agricultural adaptation, moving beyond correlation to causal inference. The result is a nuanced, historically-grounded model of cultural evolution that leverages big data to adjudicate between competing theoretical explanations.

ideas.title: Instrumental Variables from Administrative Borders to Disentangle Crop and Institutional Effects
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that observed outcomes in historical China are directly caused by crop zones by proposing that imperial administrative structures and kinship systems are powerful confounders. Drawing an analogy from health economics, where geographic discontinuities like policy borders are used as instrumental variables to isolate causal effects, the adapted research solution is to identify historical administrative borders in China that cut across relatively uniform agro-ecological zones. By employing a spatial regression discontinuity design, comparing socio-economic, cultural, or political outcomes in villages just on either side of such borders, the research can empirically disentangle the effect of persistent state institutions from the effect of the underlying crop suitability. This method provides a pathway to test whether the institutional legacy, rather than the crop itself, is the true causal factor driving long-term developmental differences.

ideas.title: Neural Ecotypes: Adapting Landscape Genetics Methods to Map Cognitive Biases in Rice-Wheat Regions
ideas.description: This idea expands the original fMRI study by drawing an analogy from landscape genetics. In that field, researchers correlate genetic variation with environmental features using spatial models to control for geographic and historical confounds. Adapting this solution, the research would treat neural connectivity patterns associated with holistic versus analytic cognition as traits to be mapped. Using historical GIS data on rice and wheat cultivation regions, spatial statistical models would be employed to correlate these neural 'ecotypes' with the dominant traditional agricultural practice. Crucially, the analysis would control for potential confounds like geographic distance between populations, modern socio-economic factors, and genetic ancestry, aiming to isolate the specific neural signature attributable to the deep cultural cognition shaped by rice or wheat farming traditions.

ideas.title: Testing the 'Irrigation-Cooperation' Hypothesis Across Agricultural Systems
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that rice farming uniquely demands cooperation by proposing a rigorous, cross-cultural test adapted from evolutionary biology. The analogous solution from behavioral ecology uses comparative phylogenetic analysis to disentangle whether a trait is uniquely tied to one lineage or is a general response to an environmental driver. Adapted to this context, the research would construct a global dataset of historical and contemporary agricultural societies, coding for primary crop type, degree of irrigation dependence, and the presence/scale of formalized labor cooperation institutions. By using a comparative framework that controls for regional history and cultural diffusion, the analysis can test whether the correlation between cooperation and irrigation holds across independent agricultural systems (e.g., wheat in Mesopotamia, maize in pre-Columbian Peru, sorghum in ancient Sudan), thereby isolating 'irrigation dependence' as the hypothesized general driver versus 'rice' as a culturally specific case.

ideas.title: Individual Differences in Holistic-Analytic Cognition: Neural Signatures and Multifactorial Origins
ideas.description: This research employs a multi-method approach to dissect the neural and experiential foundations of holistic-analytic thinking. First, individual cognitive styles are quantified using behavioral tasks and surveys, creating a continuous phenotype. Participants then undergo fMRI while performing validated tasks probing contextual dependence and field independence. The core analysis identifies neural activation and connectivity patterns that correlate with the individual behavioral style scores. Finally, a hierarchical regression model assesses the unique predictive power of regional agricultural history (rice/wheat) alongside other formative factors like urbanization, education, and personal mobility, moving beyond group averages to a nuanced, individual-differences model.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic and Ecological Testing of Reverse Causality in the Rice Theory
ideas.description: This research program directly tests the reverse causality hypothesis by integrating cultural phylogenies (e.g., from linguistic or ethnographic data) with high-resolution historical climate and soil suitability maps within Southeast Asia. By modeling whether pre-existing cultural traits predict the historical adoption and intensity of rice farming better than rice farming predicts later cultural measures, it shifts the validation goal from cross-cultural correlation to causal direction. This allows for a stronger test of the theory's core mechanism within a controlled, historically deep regional context, potentially resolving the enduring debate over agricultural determinism.

ideas.title: Disentangling Diet from Labor: A Biomarker and Intervention Approach to Staple Crop Psychology
ideas.description: This research program tests whether the nutritional properties of rice versus wheat diets influence cognitive style, independent of the social demands of their historical farming systems. It combines studies of urban migrants, using objective biomarkers to classify long-term dietary patterns, with controlled laboratory interventions that manipulate specific nutritional components. This multi-pronged approach directly addresses the fundamental confound between diet and farming practice. The goal is to isolate a causal dietary pathway, if one exists, from the well-documented socio-ecological effects of agricultural labor.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Testing of the Rice Theory: A Comparative Framework for Southeast Asia
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core solution from evolutionary biology—phylogenetic comparative analysis—to the cross-cultural validation of the rice theory. In biology, to test if a trait evolved due to an ecological pressure, researchers map traits onto a phylogenetic tree to control for shared ancestry, using methods like independent contrasts. Analogously, for testing the rice-wheat framework in India, Japan, and Indonesia, we would not treat these nations as statistically independent units. Instead, we would construct or apply existing models of cultural phylogeny (based on linguistic, genetic, or historical data) that represent the shared ancestry among these and other Southeast Asian cultures. The adapted research would then employ phylogenetic comparative methods to rigorously test whether reliance on rice farming predicts psychological outcomes (e.g., interdependence, holistic thinking) across these related cultural lineages, thereby isolating the effect of subsistence from deep historical confounds and providing a more robust validation of the theory.

ideas.title: Cultural Evolution Modeling of Agricultural-Psychological Coevolution via Epidemiological Compartment Models
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from epidemiology—specifically, compartmental models of disease spread (like SIR models)—to the problem of agricultural-psychological coevolution. In the analogous field, these models mathematically formalize how individuals move between states (e.g., susceptible, infected) based on transmission rates. The adapted idea uses this framework to model the cultural evolution of psychological traits. Farming practices (e.g., irrigation, crop rotation) are treated as transmission vectors, while populations are compartmentalized based on their psychological state regarding a trait influenced by agriculture (e.g., S: susceptible to developing high patience, I: actively adopting patience through practice, R: internalized patience as a stable trait). The model parameters would be defined by agricultural data (yield variance, labor input) and social learning rules, allowing simulation of how sustained farming environments 'infect' populations with specific psychological profiles, formalizing the gene-culture coevolution dynamic with tools from mathematical epidemiology.

ideas.title: Temporal NLP Mapping of Psychological Convergence in Urbanizing China
ideas.description: This research applies longitudinal NLP analysis to time-stamped social media data (e.g., Weibo) from users across China's historical rice and wheat zones. By tracking linguistic markers of individualism-collectivism, holistic-analytic thinking, and other psychological constructs over a decade, it can quantify the pace at which regional psychological differences are eroding. Crucially, by correlating language change with individual-level mobility data and granular urbanicity metrics, the study can test whether migration and urban exposure directly drive psychological convergence, offering a dynamic, mechanistic model of cultural evolution in real-time.

ideas.title: Causal Identification via Columbian Exchange Crop Suitability Shocks
ideas.description: This research design uses pre-colonial agro-climatic suitability for New World crops in the Old World as an instrumental variable for historical agricultural productivity. The key identification strategy leverages the Columbian Exchange as an exogenous shock that activated this latent suitability only after 1492. This isolates variation in agricultural development that is orthogonal to pre-existing regional differences in institutions and culture. The approach then traces the persistent causal effect of this post-1492 agricultural productivity shock on contemporary psychological outcomes across regions.

ideas.title: Deconstructing Subsistence Demands: A Typological and Causal Framework for Labor Coordination
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a simple crop comparison to test the specific causal link between subsistence technology and social norms of cooperation. It first develops a fine-grained typology of cooperative behaviors (e.g., simultaneous labor, conflict resolution, shared risk). This framework is then applied in a quasi-experimental design, analyzing historical and archaeological data from carefully matched pre-industrial societies practicing wet-rice, irrigated wheat, and rain-fed wheat agriculture. By isolating the variation in cooperative institutions while controlling for environmental and demographic factors, the study aims to identify which, if any, elements of cooperation are uniquely necessitated by the technical demands of paddy rice versus other irrigation-reliant systems.

papers.title: Quantum magnetism and criticality
papers.abstract: Quantum magnetism describes systems of magnetic spins in which quantum mechanical effects dominate, often in surprising ways. This review article covers phase transitions between these states, including quantum criticality and entangled electron states.

papers.title: Order and quantum phase transitions in the cuprate superconductors
papers.abstract: This is a summary of a review article appearing in Reviews of Modern Physics, July 2003. The ground state of the cuprate superconductors is described using the paradigm of competing orders. This approach has led to numerous predictions, some of which have been tested in recent nanoscale experiments.
